text_clean: I wished I had read the review before going to this site. Since I am in Lima with local, I did not bother researching, which resulted in rather disappointing experience. When you walk in, you have to wait for a guide. You cannot roam alone. We were told the next tour will start in 20 min later when we entered the site. We waited close to 30 min when the tour started. I asked if there is an English tour, and the response was 20 min later. I wish there was a set time so we did not have to sit and wait doing nothing. I decided to take the Spanish tour with my limited Spanish. I read this place up in Wiki and a few other websites night before. It is a cool place and had a great view on the top. I could not understand the majority of things guide said, and I was roaming alone among the groups. When the tour was done, there were about 5 guides sitting around there and doing nothing. I was thinking where were they before? Maybe night tour is much more popular, and all of them were gearing towards the night tour. Overall, I was disappointed at the experience.

Since I am in Lima with local, I did not bother researching, which resulted in rather disappointing experience. When you walk in, you have to wait for a guide. You cannot roam alone. We were told the next tour will start in 20 min later when we entered the site. We waited close to 30 min when the tour started. I asked if there is an English tour, and the response was 20 min later. I wish there was a set time so we did not have to sit and wait doing nothing. I decided to take the Spanish tour with my limited Spanish. I read this place up in Wiki and a few other websites night before. It is a cool place and had a great view on the top. I could not understand the majority of things guide said, and I was roaming alone among the groups. When the tour was done, there were about 5 guides sitting around there and doing nothing. I was thinking where were they before? Maybe night tour is much more popular, and all of them were gearing towards the night tour. Overall, I was disappointed at the experience.
label: negativo

text_clean: Es posible visualizar tres de las figuras desde el mirador instalado a la vera de la carretera Panamericana. Sus líneas son de unos 20 cm de ancho y el tamaño de las imágenes es fácilmente visible desde baja altura. El mantenimiento del sitio es escaso, con basura acumulada, baños en estado higiénico terrible, y tejido plástico que perturban la vista de la figura que se encuentra atravesada por la carretera. It is possible to view three of the figures from the viewpoint installed on the side of the Pan-American Highway. Its lines are about 20 cm wide and the size of the images is easily visible from low height. The maintenance of the site is scarce, with accumulated garbage, bathrooms in terrible hygienic state, and plastic fabric that disturb the view of the figure that is crossed by the road.

text_clean: Amazing place, we went there at early hours so it wasn't very crowded. Although early its foggy so beware, sometimes it takes about an hour for it to clear out. We spent about 4hrs walking around checking the whole place out, walking at steady pace and stopping at every spot you're allowed and taking photos. Because of pandemic there are guards that will tell you to "catch up with your group" if they see you staying behind. They're just doing their jobs trying to keep us safe. 🤗 There is plenty of wildlife around, big and small so keep an eye open for those too if you are interested. If you are lucky you can spot Condors sometimes flying over, but difficult to capture with phone cameras because of the sun 😔

text_clean: Si vas a viajar a Arequipa, el monasterio es una parada que si o sí te lo recomiendo. El lugar es realmente enorme, puedes estar todo el tiempo que quieras, la historia es impresionante y es un vistazo a como era la vida de las mujeres que entraban al claustro. Excelente lugar para fotos, cada rincón del monasterio es hermoso. Recomiendo que tomen la opción de ir con una guía. Me toco Laura, una señora espectacular. Su guiado fue A1, su forma de contarnos la historia del monasterio refleja la pasión por la historia y el cariño que le tiene ese lugar. Definitivamente volvería a ir, vale la pena los 45 soles de entrada. Recomendación: vayan con zapatillas cómodas porque van a caminar mucho. Usen bloqueador. Y vayan acompañados de un buen fotógrafo 😂.

Can you imagine putting your 85 year old grandmother old in the sun in the middle of the day for that long? I cannot and cannot relate to what the Peruvians are thinking!!! Obviously they are not. I consider that cruel and that I why i cannot comment except to say your handicapped policies suck!!!

text_clean: Pucllana Site Museum in Miraflores offers a fascinating glimpse into Lima’s pre-Incan history. The guided tours, included in the affordable entry fee, take you through the adobe pyramid, small museum exhibits, and a native flora and fauna area in about an hour. Its central location makes it easy to visit, and the contrast of ancient ruins amid the modern city is striking. While the site is historically significant, it’s relatively small and not as visually impressive as other Peruvian ruins. A worthwhile and educational stop if you’re nearby, but not a must-see if you’re short on time.

text_clean: The site itself is amazing, the intense crowds, inconsistent enforcement of rules (sloppily run) and guards every 100' whose job it is to just keep you off the walls and moving 1-way through the park even if that means you can't get back to your bags ruins it. This feels like the Sistine Chapel, guide you into a crowded room with 100s of people trying to sit on the ground and walls to admire, with guards quietly yet firmly yelling "no pictures!" every 30 seconds. It is hard to have a spiritual moment in this environment. We got to Machu Picchu via the 4-day Inca Trail hike and it seems like the guards don't love those folks as we walked in with day packs and walking sticks. They wouldn't let us put our bags down in the normal spot to take pictures and also blocked half our small 4 person group from getting up into the terraces to see the upper portion of MP (but we got in since no one was checking when we walked up) - this is part of the sloppiness I was referring to. My wife and I both agreed if we had bussed it up to MP and been part of the 1000s of people busing in to see it and then shuffled through it, we would have been really disappointed. Fortunately the 4 day hike we did had incredible ruins and views to see that were near private when we got to them. IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"

IMPORTANT: We both paid for tickets to climb Weynapicchu (sorry for spelling) but I bowed out after seeing it was a 1-way hike (my legs were exhausted from previous days of hiking) but my wife powered through and did it. THIS HIKE IS INTENSE. The stairs are all super narrow, you can easily fall off and die and they are very tall making scrambling the preferred method to climb them. Keep this in mind when people tell you how pretty the views are up there and you should "totally climb it"
label: negativo

text_clean: Me parece indignante que tengan cuatro o cinco llamas, una alpaca y muchos cuyes hacinados en un espacio muy pequeño, en cautiverio solo para que los turistas los vean por unos segundos. Si me hubieran informado que habría animales, no habría entrado ni apoyado ese tipo de maltrato. Es completamente innecesario tener animales en esas condiciones. No lo recomiendo. En cuanto al guía y las ruinas, todo estuvo muy bien, pero mi experiencia quedó ensombrecida por el evidente maltrato animal. Recomiendo encarecidamente que reconsideren el uso de animales en estas condiciones y busquen alternativas más éticas para enriquecer la experiencia de los visitantes, sin recurrir al sufrimiento de seres vivos.

text_clean: Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

text_clean: Lugar espectacular!! Subimos caminando desde el parque donde abrieron a las 5 am con linternas para ir iluminando el camino, habían muchas personas y pensé que era la única jaja recibir el amanecer en este lugar es algo que no tiene palabras por eso vale madrugar y ser uno de los primeros ya que para ir a wainapichu es restringida la cantidad de visitantes porque es un poco peligroso. Para llegar allí caminando fue más de una hora todo es subida, así que hay que ir bien preparado con zapatos cómodos. Se puede ir también en busitos, que recuerdo que eran como 20$ en ese momento por persona. No sé puede comer allí adentro es prohibido, las cosas son super caras así que es mejor llevar algo en un morral y ser aseados y no lanzar nada en el piso, Lo resumo un espacio mágico, estando allí en medio de sus paredes de piedra yo no podía creerlo!!

text_clean: We weren't sure if it would fit in our schedule for Peru at first, but I'm very glad we made a detour and planned some extra time for this. Absolutely stunning views and very varied flora and fauna. A few notes: the local authority Autocolca doesn't do their bookkeeping very well and I don't recommend getting E-tickets. Get your tickets in the early morning from their office in Chivay (plaza des armas) as you have the best chance of spotting the mighty condor in the morning, and they don't do much work there in the afternoon. Also I do not recommend going over the S109 (south south west) unless you have plenty of time (travel time with google maps is spot-on though) a sturdy car and good driving skills, because the road is undergoing about 100kms of heavy construction with poor driving conditions.

text_clean: What a waste of time! You go there and get tickets without a wait. The guide at the entrance who ended up being our guide during the tour told us the tour is mandatory and we have to tip her at the end. She repeated the tip part twice more during our tour but she was definitely bored and only interested in the money cause she would give us literally 1minute of basic info and then tell us to take photos while she walked on checking her phone for the entire hour! The info she gave us were marginally more than the info you read at the little room next to the ticket office and there was no explanation or contextualisation. Anyways, I felt it was a waste of time (the tour lasts one hour) and a shame she did not help us understand and appreciate more the ancient Lima culture. I was left confused because others have written amazing reviews so maybe it was just this lady guide that was really bad.

text_clean: Pasé una muy mala experiencia en la zona de ingreso a la maravilla. Soy una mujer indígena kichwa Otavaleña (Ecuador) y llegué usando mi ropa tradicional (nada extravagante ni fuera de lugar). La srta de ingreso me impedía entrar indicando que no podía ingresar vestida así, a pesar de indicarle lo anterior. Se portó antipática, nada amable. Me dijo que use una chompa para cubrir mi ropa (no llevé chompa ni nada más). También dijo algo que yo iba a hacer videos de publicidad y cosas parecidas... le dije que no era así y que si deseaba podía revisar mi celular tanto al ingreso como a la salida. Simplemente no quisieron escuchar. Luego me mandaron a retirarme de la entrada con un guardia (Sr Guaman). Al cabo de nada, empecé a sufrir una crisis acompañada de taquicardia, llamaron a primeros auxilios, quienes me acompañaron hasta estabilizarme y luego me dejaron entrar. Fue una experiencia terrible. Necesitan más capacitación y empatia, les recuerdo que el mundo está lleno de diversidad cultural, que antes éramos el Tahuantinsuyo y que compartíamos y seguimos compartiendo creencias, tradiciones, lenguas y mucho más!

text_clean: Centro historico and Plaza de Armas (Plaza Mayor) are a mandatory stop for every tourist. Being that said, it's a little disappointing. Mainly because of the insecurity you feel. I've been in other South American historical centers (e.g., Quito) and it's different. As you reach the Plaza de Armas you're told to not step one street out of the Plaza or the two main roads. The plaza itself is quite large, with Lima's Cathedral on one side and several historical buildings nearby (most of them, being remodeled). It's nice and quite crowded with tourists and people trying to sell you things.

text_clean: De reserva solo tiene el nombre y lo que te cobran en la entrada. Apenas hay carteles informativos sobre las especies y su estado de conservación, época de cría o paso por la reserva. Hay pescadores pescando con red, varias personas retirando las algas de la playa ( no sé si es por turismo o pesca) que son ecosistemas super importantes para mucha fauna que vive en ellas. Las carreteras dan lástima, los carros y bugys se meten en la playa y por las dunas ( donde viven y anidan muchas especies) y para colmo el centro de interpretación está cerrado por "mantenimiento" . El baño tampoco está incluido en el precio de la entrada. Una vergüenza de reserva, solo es un saca dinero para los turistas. Los paisajes y las playas son muy bonitas y hay muchas aves en la zona

text_clean: I came to Arequipa to enjoy the city and to visit the Monastery was something I was excited about doing. I arrived yesterday and I was extremely disappointed to find that there was no way for someone like me to enter and enjoy this beautiful piece of architecture and history.. I have muscular dystrophy and to use stairs is basically impossible for me. I understand that it is a historic city and things sometimes can’t be changed for preservation purposes - but to have no ramp or mini platform elevator or alternate entrance or even a handrail for the stairs was shocking and disappointing to say the least. I only write this review to let others know who may be physically altered (senior citizens or pregnant women) or handicapped like me that this will be what you find when you arrive there. It makes no sense for a place that is so historical and beautiful and important, a tourist treasure of the city, to not be accesible to everyone. I was still able to enjoy some of the beautiful architecture from the outside, but it was very sad not to be able to experience this.

Cuando quieres ir a ver las piletas te quieren hacer otro cobro adicional porque ahora lo han puesto “instagrameable” Los antiguos puestos de comida y postres tradicionales han sido reemplazados por locales de algodón radioactivo y pollo frito. No lo recomiendo.

text_clean: Disappointing :-( First of all, my experience has nothing to do with the actual site. The view, ruins, landscape and llamas are surreal! My disappointing experience is about the management of the site. I knew it was a touristy place and quite expensive to go. Most places are restricted/controlled and you can't enter inside the temples etc..you can just go around. Apparently a few years ago everything was open (said some friends who went before). And then you have all the tour groups that take a lot of spaces on the path. I almost didn't take any pictures, I didn't feel I had the freedom to do so because the path goes only on one way (so you can't go back on your path) and people are moving so you kind of feel a bit in a tour group without being in it and you kind of have to go forward. I didn't feel free to walk around/take pictures as I would have liked to. And then there are 4 choices of tickets, with different circuits, bit it doesn't give you access to everywhere with only one ticket, so you need to buy another one. This wasn't very cleared on the website. I just find that you put so much effort (physically, mentally and financially) to get there (waking up super early, doing all the trip to get to Aguas Calientes, walking up the (many!) stairs to the entrance, and the cost etc), in the end to leave with a bitter feeling. Is it worth it? I'm still unsure if it was.

text_clean: It's a full day trip from Lima and the flight is really only 20 minutes of viewing the lines. The bus and the lines are actually not so bad, but what people forget to consider is that the planes are 6 seaters and are really shaky and induce dizziness so the experience itself is an exercise in trying to squint to see the lines while not vommitting. The town itself has a couple worthwhile eateries, but I wouldn't spend the night other than to break up the trip.

text_clean: The site is amazing and well worth a visit, but you need to be aware about a few "roadblocks". Tickets can only be booked online. On site they are only selling cable car tickets. There is only one poor person to help you with the entrance buying process managed by the Ministery of Culture. Once you finally have all your tickets, you need to wait for the bus to take you to the cable car station (10min), and then there are 20min of a very scenic trip before reaching the path leading to the site (30mins walk, horses available). Once you reached the site, there are guides but you better speak Spanish :-) There is a guide who learned a little English and he is doing his best but communication will be difficult. That being said, do not miss i!!. We don't know much about the people who built this place because Incas conquered the city 70 years before Conquistadors burned it, and the site is extremely interesting.

text_clean: It was an amazing experience and although it was very busy it wasn't crowded as you can see in the pictures. Fyi, the pictures do not do the site justice. Its fantastic!! You can walk up if you feel you need a challenge or you can go up in a bus from the town of Machu Picchu. The queue looks horrendous but it moves quickly and takes about half an hour or so. Up at the top there is loo and you can store luggage so you dont have to drag it up. The start is pretty strenuous and steep with about 200 hundred steps straight up (which is more difficult if you've not yet fully acclimatised to the altitude). From then on it's all a bit easier and the views leave you breathless (as well as the altitude). I'd say that you need to bring lots of water with you because you dehydrate quickly in the heat and strong sun (husband and I brought two litres which wasn't enough). You also definitely need sunblock unless you don't mind burning to a crisp. We saw no evidence of mosquitos! It is also pretty normal for it to rain and thunder so you'll need to come prepared for that. You can visit without a guide but I think you'll not get to appreciate the site as much. At the end you can also walk back down to the town or again just take the bus. Again the queue looks bad but moves very quick.

text_clean: I really hate 1 star reviews for this historical site but I am writing one. Due to the protests in September of 2025 we were unable to get our train to Aguas Caliente. I completely support people's right to protest and was unwilling to travel to MP during this dispute. Some people travelled by van for 5 hours and walked for several more miles to access the town. I chose to stay in Ollantaytambo for a couple of extra days which was really enjoyable. I am giving MP 1 star because despite the issues people have accessing MP their refund process is impossible. I was told I need to submit personal information including pictures of my passport. They sent me a link to do this. The link does not say anything about the purpose of why I am submitting this information. I am wary of providing this information without a guarantee of getting a refund. The ticketing website has no information about asking for refunds. Surely they are aware that thousands of people were unable to go to MP due to the strike. Perurail actually sent an email explaining the refund process. I imagine the ministry of tourism made quite a bit of money from no-show tickets.

text_clean: Esta junto a la playa " El Raspon" y las caracteristicas son similares, esta ubicada en el Area de conservacion protegida de la peninsula de Paracas ( Dpto. De Ica ). La via de acceso tiene pesima señalizacion y es insegura. Es una limpia playa de arena y agua agradable, el nombre de la playa se debe a la existencia del yacimiento de carbon del cual se servian los lugareños en la antiguedad, hay que decender aprox. 35 mts. Por escalinatas de concreto bien construidas para llegar a la playa , se puede observar fauna marina y aves guaneras, existen formaciones geologicas y cavidades producidas por la erosion marina y vientos , en estas se observan tambien restos fosiles de flora y fauna asi como capas de diferentes etapas de formacion geologica. Por el tema del decenso a la playa; no recomiendo estas playas para las personas con discapacidad en las piernas o de edad muy avanzada. Hay estacionamiento y seguridad

text_clean: I visited the archaeological site today, and although the tour was interesting, I was shocked by the living standards of the animals on the site, present to illustrate the resources of the ancient people. There were ducks with no access to fresh water, which is part of their essential living environment - instead they had a stagnant, filthy puddle and were surrounded by their own waste. The guinea pigs were also living in tiny pens, covered in their own waste. I would ask the site management to please rethink the provisions they are giving to these animals - perhaps even charging a slightly higher ticket price to allow for a better environment. The current living standards are really not appropriate, and lets down an otherwise good value tour.

text_clean: El pueblo de aguas Calientes me pareció hermoso, me parece que es un lugar bastante hermoso pero exageradamente costoso y con un poco de falta de cultura, al caminar pude ver cómo un lugar de estos que es patrimonio de la humanidad se encuentra lleno de basura y contaminación tanto por los locales como por los mismos turistas que van a visitar a este bello lugar, también es un lugar en el que todo te lo quieren cobrar en dólares y conseguir boletería es bastante complejo y difícil de conseguir, en ocasiones no se consigue ni estando en el mismo lugar, mi forma de ver a Machu Picchu fue el subir una de las montañas que está al frente, una ruta hermosa pero algo complicada debido a que cortaron los anclajes y escaleras, solo quedan dos cuerdas y dos Guayas que sirven de ayuda el resto toca escalar bajo su propio riesgo. La forma fácil de ir por su propia cuenta es comprar un tiquete en la terminal terrestre que va a Quillabamba y tienes que bajarte en santa María y que tiene un valor de 25 soles por persona, el bus se toma en la terminal de Quillabamba y un taxi te cobra 6 soles, en santa María se toma un colectivo hasta santa Teresa que te cobrará 10 soles por persona y de santa Teresa a hidro electrica te cobrarán 5 soles por persona y de ahí solo queda caminar hasta llegar al pueblo de aguas calientes.

text_clean: Un lugar mágico y espléndido. Vale la pena ir. El mejor circuito es el #2, largo y te da varias vistas. Nosotros ingresamos a las 6 am y fue muy fácil, mejor llegar en tren y no caminando, la ruta del Inca es de 2 horas ida y 2 horas vuelta. Fuimos en Octubre y era temporada baja, el mismo día que llegamos a Aguas calientes compramos el boleto de ingreso a Machu sin reserva, sin problemas, hay un descuento para los colombianos por ser comunidad Andina. text_clean: Fuimos a la salida de un curso el día domingo, y esta fue mi segunda vez asistiendo. Sin embargo, por segunda vez consecutiva noté los mismos detalles que deberían mejorar. Los recorridos son demasiado planos y se vuelven algo monótonos, además de que los grupos son muy grandes, lo que hace que no todos los visitantes presten atención o se sientan incluidos. En mi caso, la señorita que guiaba avanzaba muy rápido y parecía dirigirse solo a un grupo reducido de personas, dejando de lado a los demás, sin atender las dudas que teníamos. También avanzaba a su propio ritmo, sin esperar que el resto del grupo se reagrupe, lo cual me pareció poco considerado. Fuera de esos puntos, el lugar en sí está muy bien cuidado. Se nota el cariño y el buen manejo que tienen, ya que todo se ve limpio, ordenado y en excelente estado. Aunque todo está muy bien conservado, hay ciertas partes como la zona final del recorrido, en la parte más alta de la huaca endonde se sienten algunos tramos un poco peligrosos, sobre todo al descender, ya que hay curvas pronunciadas sin barandas o elementos de seguridad que podrían evitar accidentes. En general, es un lugar valioso y bien preservado, pero el servicio de guiado y la seguridad en algunos tramos podrían mejorar mucho la experiencia del visitante.

text_clean: Visitar el Monasterio de Santa Catalina fue, sin exagerar, una de las experiencias más fascinantes que he vivido en Perú. No solo por su belleza arquitectónica, sino por la atmósfera que se respira en cada rincón: una mezcla envolvente de historia, silencio, espiritualidad y arte. Desde el primer momento que crucé sus enormes puertas de piedra, sentí que estaba entrando a otra época. El monasterio es una ciudadela en sí misma, con sus propias calles, pequeñas plazas, fuentes, cocinas, lavanderías y celdas que, en su momento, fueron ocupadas por monjas de clausura. Caminando por los pasillos de piedra volcánica, se puede sentir el aislamiento deliberado del mundo exterior y la devoción con la que vivían allí. Los colores de las paredes son vibrantes: el rojo terracota, el azul profundo y el blanco encalado contrastan perfectamente con la luz intensa del cielo arequipeño. Cada rincón es fotogénico, pero más allá de eso, todo parece cuidadosamente pensado para fomentar la contemplación. A pesar de que ahora es un lugar abierto al público, el silencio sigue predominando, como si el monasterio aún guardara los secretos de las mujeres que vivieron y murieron ahí. Una de las cosas que más me impresionó fue la celda de Sor Ana de los Ángeles Monteagudo, una beata arequipeña cuya habitación permanece casi intacta. También me llamó la atención la sencillez de los objetos que usaban, contrastando con el lujo con el que muchas de estas monjas, provenientes de familias aristocráticas, llegaron al monasterio. La visita guiada (altamente recomendable) me permitió entender mejor la historia del lugar, que data del siglo XVI. El guía explicó con claridad cómo se fue expandiendo el monasterio, cómo cambiaron las reglas de clausura con los años, y cómo ahora una pequeña comunidad de monjas aún vive en una zona restringida del recinto, separada del área turística. En definitiva, el Monasterio de Santa Catalina no es solo un monumento histórico, es una cápsula del tiempo. Me fui con la sensación de haber conocido un pedazo profundo de la historia colonial del Perú, pero también con una extraña paz interior. Es un lugar que invita a caminar despacio, a observar, y a reflexionar. Una joya imperdible en el corazón de Arequipa.

text_clean: La Plaza Mayor de Lima, también conocida como Plaza de Armas, es el corazón histórico y simbólico de la ciudad. Rodeada por imponentes edificios como la Catedral de Lima, el Palacio de Gobierno y la Municipalidad, ofrece un escenario único que refleja el esplendor colonial y la importancia política del Perú. Su amplitud, la cuidada jardinería y la majestuosidad de sus construcciones la convierten en un espacio muy fotogénico y siempre lleno de vida, tanto de turistas como de locales. Aunque los alrededores pueden ser un poco caóticos y con presencia de comercio informal, la plaza en sí mantiene su encanto y seguridad, siendo un lugar imprescindible para quienes visitan Lima. Desde aquí parte el famoso Jirón de la Unión, que conecta con la Plaza San Martín, ofreciendo un recorrido lleno de historia, arquitectura y tradición. En definitiva, es un sitio de visita obligada para comprender y disfrutar el alma del centro histórico limeño.

Absolutely watch the changing of the guard ceremony, the Republican Guard band played Despecito. Exchanging money on the street in this area is generally safe but ai would not recommend doing that outside here (unless at a bank, hotel, or well established business). One of my favorite places in Lima, great food and amazing history.

text_clean: Extremely disappointing. I was wanting to come here for 20 years and when I finally got here, it was the ultimate let down as everywhere else in Latin America they have created a Latin America Disneyland. The town of aguas caliante is the WORST tourist town I've ever seen, extremely over priced, terrible quality on everything and all just to see some royal city built by slaves to be abandoned after 80 years. This is what happens when you let the normal person choose the 'new seven wonders of the world'. The ticketing system makes no sense and don't expect anyone to help you.The Salkantay trek here Is by far more adventurous, more eye opening and more enjoyable than the anticlimactic town of aguas caliante and machu Pichu. It's such a shame the Peruvian people have let this happen.

text_clean: Visited on 4/24/24, which was my birthday, and it was one of the most epic experiences of my life. It was so amazing that we decided to go back the following day as well. There’s little that hasn’t already been said about this location and the history is well known so I will spare that part of my review but honestly, this is a destination that everyone should visit at least once in their life. I saw very young people all the way up to people in their 90s so it really is possible for anyone except those with mobility or health issues. The mystery and beauty of Machu Picchu are reasons enough to visit, but just being there and walking the grounds you really feel connected to history and the views are absolutely breathtaking. Our first day we went with a guide and the second time we went by ourselves. Both were great experiences. You can walk up to the entrance of Machu Picchu but I would suggest taking a bus because it will take multiple hours to get up there. You can also take the Inka Trail (3 nights and 4 days) and hike down into Machu Picchu so there are multiple different methods of getting there. We decided to stay in Aguas Calientes for a few nights so we took the bus to and from each visit. Our first day we went with a guide who took us around but the second day we decided to choose Circuit 2 which many have said is the best route. It is definitely the most comprehensive and you get to see most of the site this way. We also added the Inka Bridge which was closed when we visited, but we were able to get very close to it. It’s an extra half mile climb once you reach the top of Machu Picchu but honestly it’s pretty easy, however, I would avoid it if you are afraid of heights because the pathway there is very narrow and the drop off to the side is hundreds, if not over 1000, feet to the bottom. I am still in awe of everything I saw and it really left me with an immense appreciation and respect for the Peruvian people and what the Inca accomplished. I could not recommend this site enough and feel that it definitely deserves the UNESCO world heritage site badge as well as being one of the new Seven Wonders of the World.

text_clean: Es una plaza bonita, amplia pero le falta más zonas verdes, está rodeada por la Casa de Gobierno, el Arzobispado, la Catedral, Municipalidad de Lima y negocios todos hermosas edificaciones antiguas y bien cuidadas, lamentablemente no se puede apreciar hacia más de un año que casi se ma tiene cerrada la policía no deja que entren según dice que por seguridad y eso impide el turismo y los negocios cercanos de artesanías sufren las consecuencias. Hay un caballo con una carreta que pasean al rededor no se cuánto cobran, cerca está la Iglesia de San Francisco donde se puede hacer un recorrido pagado por las catacumbas, también está cerca la Alameda Chabuca Granda un lugar que fue rescatado de la delincuencia, ahora encuentras bastantes carritos de venta de comida y postres típicos muy ricos y de precio cómodos también puedes ver grupos de música, baile y si es de tu gusto los cómicos ambulantes. Hay vigilancia pero de todas maneras hay que tener cuidado muy visitado por turistas en un solo lugar encuentras gran variedad de la gastronomía peruana sobre todo limeña. No recomiendo ir fin de semana hay muchísima gente.

text_clean: This is a summary of an August visit to Apu Machu Picchu (towards the end of peak season). If you’ve spent at least two days in Cusco, a night in Aguas Calientes, and stayed well hydrated, altitude should not be a problem. I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.

text_clean: It is one of those Once in a Lifetime kind of experiences, especially if you are traveling from Asia, it is so far to get to and it was almost 3 days of traveling before we actually set foot on Machu Picchu! Most people likely to fly into Cusco and somehow then find their way either by car or train to Aguas Calientes which is the town at the foothill of Machu Picchu. In fact it is also where most people will spend a night if you are not staying at the one and only hotel at the top, which is the Belmond Sanctuary. From Aguas Caliente, across the bridge from the train station and there’s where you will catch a bus up to Machu Picchu. The bus ride is only a short 15min through the winding road uphill or it is possible to hike up which may take you an hour. The current (as of June 18) entrance rule to the Machu Picchu is that there are 2 sessions a day from 6am-12pm and again 12pm-5:30pm. With each entrance ticket, you will get access to the park twice (in and out) as there are no toilet and food service inside the park, you will have to come out and go back in. Strongly recommend that you bring snacks and water as even outside the park, your food options are very limited with extremely long lines. The morning session is significantly more crowded than the afternoon. Since the Incan worship the sun, many visitors like to witness sunrise over Machu Picchu. In fact, you don't really get to see Sunrise as it is blocked by the surrounding mountains, but what you will see (on a clear day) is when the sunlight hits the ancient ruin, which is still a sight to behold. However, if you were only to do 1 entry (we did both the morning and afternoon), I would recommend the afternoon session as the site cleared out by 3pm and you almost have the entire place to yourself. It is then peaceful and quiet. Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.

Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.
label: neutro

To finally arrive at the Sun Gate and see Machu Picchu for the first time, at dawn, just, wow. The amusing part is then walking around this breathtaking place, after 5 days of basically living rough, filthy and covered in bug bites amongst all the clean tourists who have got the train up, makes you feel quite self conscious lol!! destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: n un desierto de Nazca, se encuentran estos enigmáticos dibujos y líneas que los arqueólogos las denominan Geogliflos y que hasta el día de hoy no encuentran una explicación del como y el porque fueron construidos, solo como un detalle no son visibles desde el nivel del suelo, fueron hechos para ser vistos desde lo alto y las evidencias de las culturas de esa época, evidenciaban de que hubieran necesitado tecnologías superiores que no les correspondería ni a la época ni lugar. Según información de nuestro guía, las líneas rectas son tan perfectas que su desviación geométrica medida, no llegan al 2 por 1000 (2 metros por cada kilómetro) La Líneas de Nazca más representativas son los figuras de animales, entre ellos: colibríes, cóndores, garza, la grulla, el pelícano, gaviota, mono, araña, caracol, perro con patas y cola larga, figura antropomorfa, dos llamas, etc. Reptiles como el lagarto, cortado por la carretera Panamericana Sur, la iguana, lagartija, y una serpiente. Además de Líneas rectas a manera de pistas de aterrizaje, mezclado con espirales. Su perfecto estado de conservación se debe María Reiche Neumann, alemana que dedico su vida a limpiar y literalmente barrer las líneas para evitar su destrucción y es gracias a ella que la Unesco inscribió a “ Las líneas y Geogliflos de Nazca y de Pampas de Jumana” como Patrimonio de la Humanidad.

text_clean: Todo peruano debe conocer este lugar!!! Es excelente y sobre todo barato... Si vienes desde Chachapoyas puedes tomar tours q cobran entre 90 a 110 soles x persona todo incluido, hasta almuerzos... Por cuenta propia tomas una combi a las 6am a 15 soles aprox hasta el distrito de Nuevo Tingo, luego caminas 15 minutos hasta el local de embarque de transporte para el ingreso hacia los TELEFÉRICOS donde compras tú entrada (20.40 soles precio, incluye ida y vuelta) IMPORTANTE.. NO PERDER LOS TICKETS xq si los pierdes, compras otros para el regreso al precio inicial... una vez pagado, esperas tú turno y ellos te llevan en bus hasta la zona elevada del cerro para subir por fin a los telefericos, el trayecto es de 20 minutos aprox, luego te hacerlas a la casa amarilla donde pagas tú entrada a 30 soles y te permiten subir el camino de 2 km q demoran unos 45 minutos... Ya en la cima vaz a la derecha hasta el ingreso a la fortaleza donde entregas la entrada a compraste a 30 soles... Una vez adentro, DISFRUTA DEL PASEO, debes calcular tus tiempos xq el ticket del teleférico te menciona la hora de regreso... Así q atento... Igual te dan tolerancia pero si hay mucha gente puede q se compliquen los tiempos pues la cola se hace grande al regreso. Pásenla chvrs causitas! Sí desean alguien que les guíe en el tour les dejo el número de contacto del guía que sale en la foto de chaleco beige

text_clean: Este Circuito es uno de los más hermosos de toda Latinoamérica. Para mí la fuente principal y mas hermosa e impactante es la más grande de forma corcular de esta lindo parque de la reserva, en esta oportunidad me tocó asistir un día sábado y es un día que por más que sea fin de semana no lo recomiendo para asistir. Es un día súper repleto de personas y para entrar hay dos entradas para pagar en taquilla, el precio es de 4 soles por personas y las personas con discapacidad pueden ingresar pagando el 50% de la entrada, por ser un día súper difícil de asistir y de noche, para el ingreso se hacen unas colas gigantes, en donde hay usureros que se ponen en la entrada del parque a venderte los puestos para acercarte más en la entrada. Es una completa locura no me gustó asistir este día. Tuve que correr para la otra entrada antes de que se volviera a poner mas larga. Lo único que deberían hacer es aprovechar las taquillas vacías para colocar cajeras en los días que son más concurridos por personas residenciales y turistas. Es la única cosa negativa. text_clean: Bueno a ver es un sitio que si estais en Miraflores teneis que conocer. Solo que no me gustaron varias cosas. Bueno la visita al museo tiene una duración de una hora y no puedes hacerlo por tu cuenta. Es con un guia. Según la hora que vengas hay dos tours en inglés o español para la visita guiada. Que no lo hagas por tu cuenta, me refiero que quizas podría poner un tour de 30min. Quizas no quiero estar una hora expuesto al sol. Por otro lado la entrada es muy económica y apta para todos los bolsillos, pero yo pague para ver el museo histórico y al final del tour te llevan a ver los animales tipicos de Perú, encerrados... Después otro detalle fui al baño al acabar el tour y no habia agua ni en los grifos ni cisterna... Creo que este tipo de sitios tendría que estar adecuado y adaptado en todo. Por lo demas si lo recomiendo visitar, pero quitaría lo de Zoo, al menos avisar cuando compras la entrada de que se trata y tambien un tour express de 30min. Asi que lo dejo en 4 estrellas. Creo que es comprensible.

It was suggested that although you can walk up from the town you may be better taking the 35 min bus ride. I’m glad we did. The buses run all day every 10-15min so the queue does move quickly but def start to queue an hour before your entry time. DONT FORGET YOUR PASSPORT! It’s not a difficult hike / walk if you doing the main area but pack water, rain jacket and sun protection. Only small bags allowed. No walking sticks (but they really are not needed). The tickets are +- $25 per entry. We got a guide at the entrance 100sol per person. She was amazingly knowledgeable and we were with her for 3hrs. Money well spent - we came away appreciating the experience even more. We had been giving the suggestion of route 2 that goes a bit higher up than route 1 but still covers most of route 1. Guide confirmed it was a good choice. We going back tomorrow to do Wayna Picchu mountain ($50 per entry for this hike) and will do route 4 before climbing the mountain (which you do need to be fit and prepared for). FYI - The temple of the moon is still closed since Covid as of today. I’m glad that we had two days entry as the 2nd morning the whole area was covered in cloud so you couldn’t see the site but we waited at the top of the mountain and it cleared for a beautiful view. There are some bad reviews of routes limiting access but you can see from my photos that it also means your visit won’t look like a market and you can get some lovely photos and enjoy the experience. Some interesting bits I learnt today sharing if interested: The rocks that make Machu Picchu came from part of the mountain that broke off so the area was a quarry of rocks. You can see some of the bigger rocks that make up the Fertility Temple are actually rocks that go right into the mountain. Machu Picchu sits on a mountain made of different types of quartz which is good energy. Hope you enjoy your visit and take a moment to feel the vibes.

text_clean: De las mejores playas del Perú: limpia, calmada. Agua muy clara en comparación con las demás playas del país (incluida Máncora). En épocas de verano en Lima, hay concurrencia masiva hacia esta playa. Me comentaron que en otras épocas hay menos gente, lo cual lo hace más agradable si no te agrada el tumulto. Sin embargo, aún con mucha gente es una playa totalmente recomendable. Si haríamos una comparación con Máncora (Piura) diría que Máncora es un tanto "exclusiva" y La Mina es "limpia". Eso sí NO ES POSIBLE ACAMPAR en este lugar. Si deseas acampar te recomiendo la playa Lagunillas, a unos 10 minutos en auto. El transporte no es un problema.

text_clean: English y español (second paragraph). Be careful with this tourist trap, you will need a reservation to go in, however, this is not well explained on the normal internet search. Here is the scheme, a lot of tourists, mostly English speaking come without reservation and there are a couple of ”tourists guides", taxi drivers in reality impersonating being part of the museum staff, at the entrance letting you know that you will not be able to enter without the reservation. In exchange, they can take to another archeological site for a significant amount of money (varies from 400 to 250 PENS). If you try to make it to the reception window the taxi driver cut you off and let them know that you don't have reservations with the typical wink wink, so they don't do much to help you. At the end, if there are space available or no shows, you might get access to the museum. So, my recommendation is, if you don't have a reservation, do not even bother to show up. Now, the museum inside is ok, you will learn about the pre-inka civilization in the area and their worship to the god of the sea. The your is mandatory, it last just one hour, and is quite entertaining, if you are into this kind of attractions. Cuidado con esta trampa para turistas. TIENE que tener reservación para entrar al museo. Si no tiene reservación vas a encontrar a un par de buitres con carnet de taxistas haciéndose pasar por guías turísticos y empleados del museo (que no son) para llevarte a otros sitios arqueológicos a 30 minutos en carro por una suma considerable suma (250 a 400 soles). Los taxistas se comunican con los empleados del museo para dejarlos saber que no tienes reserva con el típico guiño de ojo para que no traten de ayudarte mucho para clavarte con el tour del taxista. Al final, so hay cupo y presentas presión, en el museo te venden los tickets, pero eso es ya cosa de suerte. Asi, que si no tienes reservación, ni trates de llegar al museo, a menos que te quieras correr la suerte. Ahora, ya dentro del museo, el tour es mandatario, y esta muy entretenido. Sólo dura una hora.

text_clean: 6-7 hour bus ride from Arequipa (terminal terrestre, Arturo Ibanez 130) to Cabanaconde. Route over highlands, vicuna reserve, down to Chivay in canyon, then down canyon, (best views from right side of bus). 9:30 AM Arequipa departure (Andelusia) and nights in Cabanaconde before and after self guided trek, seemed better than 3:00 AM Arequipa departure, short last trek day and back to Arequipa of tours. Many places to stay, I was at Pachamama hostel about 2 blocks up from the plaza on San Pedro St. Good information about trek options and good place to meet others doing unguided treks. I did 2d/1n trek, 1st day to Llahuar with scenic descent along canyon edge, then down into side canyon, final descent to Colca river crossing, climb into Taruna, back down to Llahuar on the Colca river just after crossing the Huaruro river. Nice bungalows on river, friendly staff, fixed menu dinner, breakfast and sandwichs available, all at very reasonable prices. Natural hot pools and swimming pool right on river not open because river very high in early March. Very ambitious new "restaurant" construction ongoing. As hike to Llahuar mostly down and not too long, I was there well before noon. Could just chill in nice location. I opted for day hike up Huaruro canyon that starts steeply right from Llahuar then more gradually up to Llatica where a bridge crosses the river. From there another steep climb to high trail that contours back down other side of canyon to above Belen. Nice safe and scenic canyon trails. Down main street in Belen past soccer field to steep trail to Paclla. Cross road and continue on trail down through terraced gardens that eventually goes to Taruna above Llahuar. This added another 11 km and 700m vertical, about 4 hours at a fast pace, to first day. 2nd day back up to Belen, options are trail short cuts to Paclla not too steep, then very steep to Belen, or longer but more gradually walk on road. From Belen you are on road up to viewpoint bend then level to shortcut trail down to plateau, join main trail down to Sangalle (relatively touristy and expensive, but amazing given that everything has to come in by horse or burro). That just leaves the 2.5 - 3.5 hour all uphill switchbacks to plateau and final km to Cabanaconde. Total for 2nd day a healthy 16-18km with 1700m elevation gain. If I was doing it again, would spend 2 nights in canyon, 1st at Llahuar, then 2nd up at Fure with day hike up to the waterfall (cataratas), day 3 high trail back from Fure contouring back to join road at high point above Sangalle, then through Sangalle up and out. Another interesting high altitude strenuous option might be continuing up from Fure to Tocallo at 3800m where some maps show a trail that goes in and out of several canyons with final pass then long descent to Malata above Sangalle. Another night in canyon and some local information about condition of trails would be needed. Canyon country was unique, scenic and very green with lots of water in the rainy season. Lodging and meals in many areas down in canyon allows for very light packs, a plus given all the climbs. No reservations needed for anything during rainy season, probably a different story in high season. A minibus service that goes on the not too scary dirt road from Carbanaconde, crossing the Rio Colca at the same place my trek did has beautiful brand new rain sheltered stops at all the little towns all the way to Tapay! This allows some less strenuous options for the canyon that are sure to become more popular.

text_clean: ¡Wow! Este lugar es mágico. El nombre original es Pacallaqta. Si no conocen, vale la pena ir en un tour organizado por INCARAIL (ofrecen paquetes directamente con ellos y sus trenes, eviten intermediarios innecesarios y posibles estafas). Este lugar tiene mucha paz, si encuentran buen clima, se disfruta todo: la vista, la vegetación, el esparcimiento, los rincones en cada espacio, la fauna, todo. No es pesado para subir, y tampoco es cansado recorrerlo, ya que una vez llegado a la cima, todo es en descenso. Huayna Picchu es la montaña que está detrás, para poder acceder se requiere un pago especial con reserva previa, mucha condición y experiencia, porque es pesado y peligroso por la altura y la inclinación. Las dudas sobre su construcción y sobre lo que "falta" por descubrir, mantienen este lugar enigmático.

Las dudas sobre su construcción y sobre lo que "falta" por descubrir, mantienen este lugar enigmático.
label: negativo

text_clean: Machu Picchu is an ancient Incan citadel located high in the Andes Mountains of Peru. The site is one of the most iconic and breathtakingly beautiful destinations in the world, attracting millions of visitors every year. To reach Machu Picchu, visitors can either hike the famous Inca Trail or take a train from the nearby town of Cusco. Once there, visitors can explore the ruins of the citadel, which include impressive structures such as temples, terraces, and plazas. The intricate stonework and engineering feats of the Incas are truly awe-inspiring. While Machu Picchu can get crowded, especially during peak season, there are ways to avoid the crowds and make the most of the experience. One option is to visit early in the morning or later in the afternoon when there are fewer visitors. Another option is to take a guided tour, which can provide insight into the history and significance of the site. Overall, Machu Picchu is a must-see destination for anyone visiting Peru or South America. The beauty, history, and cultural significance of the site are truly unmatched, and it's an experience that will stay with you for a lifetime. Una vez allí, los visitantes pueden explorar las ruinas de la ciudadela, que incluyen impresionantes estructuras como templos, terrazas y plazas. La intrincada obra de piedra y los logros de ingeniería de los incas son realmente impresionantes. En general, Machu Picchu es un destino imperdible para cualquier persona que visite Perú o América del Sur. Otra opción es tomar un recorrido guiado, que puede proporcionar información sobre la historia y la importancia del sitio. En general, Machu Picchu es un destino imperdible para cualquier persona que visite Perú o América del Sur. La belleza, la historia y la importancia cultural del sitio son realmente incomparables, y es una experiencia que permanecerá contigo para toda la vida.
label: neutro

text_clean: Totally worth the hype. Amazing to see in person. Tips we wished we’d known: Get there at 6 for the misty views that then open up into clear sights. Stay for a few hours at one of the viewpoints because it’s a one-way, no re-entry system. Go for circuit 2 for the best views. Get to the bus stop early as the queues start an hour before. The weather changes super fast so pack rain gear. You can book your Machu Picchu ticket MUCH cheaper on the website rather than through a tour. Either bring plenty of money for food as it’s very expensive or bring pack-ups. Go to the cascades for a swim while in aguas calientes. There are 1000 tickets released daily for walk-ins, it opens at 6am but people were queuing before 5am. Remember to bring your passports for both the train and the entrance—they check multiple times (you can even get a passport stamp!). The bus to get there is 12 USD per person each way, or it is a steep uphill climb which takes about 1hr 40min. We got the bus up and walked down.

SECOND: It is a beautiful place and photographs don't make it justice. We saw it in about 3 hours ( in the afternoon).

text_clean: El pueblo de aguas Calientes me pareció hermoso, me parece que es un lugar bastante hermoso pero exageradamente costoso y con un poco de falta de cultura, al caminar pude ver cómo un lugar de estos que es patrimonio de la humanidad se encuentra lleno de basura y contaminación tanto por los locales como por los mismos turistas que van a visitar a este bello lugar, también es un lugar en el que todo te lo quieren cobrar en dólares y conseguir boletería es bastante complejo y difícil de conseguir, en ocasiones no se consigue ni estando en el mismo lugar, mi forma de ver a Machu Picchu fue el subir una de las montañas que está al frente, una ruta hermosa pero algo complicada debido a que cortaron los anclajes y escaleras, solo quedan dos cuerdas y dos Guayas que sirven de ayuda el resto toca escalar bajo su propio riesgo. La forma fácil de ir por su propia cuenta es comprar un tiquete en la terminal terrestre que va a Quillabamba y tienes que bajarte en santa María y que tiene un valor de 25 soles por persona, el bus se toma en la terminal de Quillabamba y un taxi te cobra 6 soles, en santa María se toma un colectivo hasta santa Teresa que te cobrará 10 soles por persona y de santa Teresa a hidro electrica te cobrarán 5 soles por persona y de ahí solo queda caminar hasta llegar al pueblo de aguas calientes.

text_clean: Es la llaqta fortificada más importante del nororiente peruano. Se caracteriza por su arquitectura única, con una imponente mampostería que alcanza los 20 metros de altura. Se encuentra a 3000 metros sobre el nivel del mar. Se encuentra a una hora desde el poblado de Chachapoyas, llegar donde se toma el teleférico que lo sube por 30 minutos hasta Kuelap el circuito puede ser de una hora a 3 horas. Las agencias cobran el tour 90 soles el cual incluye el bus desde Chachapoyas los pasajes en teleférico, almuerzo, y guía. Se recomienda visitar el lugar aparte de ser impresionante se puede apreciar varios micro climas.

El costo de entrada para adultos es de 40 soles, pero es genial que los niños menores de 2 años y los adultos mayores de 65 años no tengan que pagar entrada. El lugar cuenta con diferentes ambientes, baños convenientemente ubicados y tachos de basura en todas partes para mantenerlo limpio. El paisaje es verdaderamente hermoso, con numerosos jardines y fuentes que añaden un toque de serenidad al lugar. Definitivamente, es un destino que recomendaría para una visita en familia.

text_clean: A great wonder of the world! Fascinating views and doesn’t seem real. Before going there, make sure you are used to the altitude, ensure that you have some bug spray and sun screen when you go. The usual touristy hike is not bad, just 20 min up the stairs, and I suggest taking some breaks on here, then a lot of flat and downhill land. I suggest nothing more than a light snack, and make sure you keep the trash in your own bag. There is no trash cans, no water fountains, and no bathrooms except before you enter the ticketing booth line or after. Please be respectful as this is a historical sight!

text_clean: La Plaza Mayor de Lima es simplemente impresionante. Un lugar lleno de historia, arquitectura y vida. Rodeada por la Catedral, el Palacio de Gobierno y edificios coloniales hermosos, caminar por aquí es sentir el corazón del Perú latiendo fuerte. La plaza está bien cuidada, limpia y con jardines preciosos que invitan a sentarse, observar y disfrutar el ambiente. Siempre hay movimiento: turistas, locales, artistas y ceremonias oficiales que le dan un aire especial. Ideal para fotos, para aprender historia y para conectarse con la esencia de Lima. Visita obligada si vienes a la ciudad, tanto de día como de noche.

text_clean: Vengo desde que se inauguró el Circuito y dejé de venir hace años, hoy 01/03/2026 regresé con mi familia con la esperanza de pasarla bonito como años anteriores y pasear pero todo es distinto. 1. El aforo parece que sobrepasa, hay gente en exceso ya ni se puede pasear ni caminar agusto. 2. No sé desde cuándo ya no se puede pasar por el túnel hacia el otro parque sin que pagues S/5 + OBLIGATORIAMENTE, años anteriores podías pasar y visitar ambos parques pagando solo 1 entrada. Eso me parece un abuso, ni siquiera está indicado en la boletería. O sea, si no quiero pasar por ese evento X que hay y decoran porque simplemente no me gusta se tiene que cancelar solo para pasar al otro parque?! 👎🏽 3. Los baños están en pésimo estado, no hay cerrojo en las puertas en la mayoría y la palanca del agua del inodoro NO FUNCIONA, por S/1.50 cada persona, fácil se puede arreglar eso en cuestión de horas pero otras personas me indican que está así ya tiempo y una señorita apurada trata de limpiar los baños pero no es así no se trata de eso. 4. Este parque se ha vuelto como un centro comercial, hay demasiados juegos electrónicos para niños (no está mal que haya pero es demasiado) y venta de muchas cosas, NO se disfruta de las piletas que contando hay como 6 en el parque que permiten entrar.

text_clean: Es la plaza principal de Lima, muy hermosa, se puede visitar a toda hora si es que no hay alguna manifestación pública porque lo cierran. text_clean: Hands down a must, for those interested in History Of Culture and Architecture, and natural beauty of Peaks and Valleys. It’s really a place of miracles, how the Incas designed and built what they did is mind blowing. Suggest, walking up to the Ruins from town rather then the bus, it’s couple hour walk at a slow pace, gorgeous way to transition into the ruins, maybe take a bus back to town or visa versa. Stay there if you can, but the hotel at the Ruins does gets booked, so you can stay in town. But there are little peaks and Inca trails to explore at the Ruins as well. Great photo opts and place for Spiritual Seekers, even amongst the crowds!

text_clean: Yo, this is a once-in-a-lifetime experience, but it's a long day if you stay in the Sacred Valley. But trust me, it's totally worth it. The hike is moderate, so take your time and hike slowly. Machu Picchu isn't going anywhere, it can wait for you. Remember to bring some small change, because there's only one toilet at the entrance and you have to pay to use it. The line can be super long, and sometimes they even run out of water, even though it's a paid toilet. So, bring some wet wipes and your own toilet paper, just in case. That's the only downside, really.

text_clean: Fue la razón de mi viaje a Perú. Subí dos veces en diferentes dias. Un dia compartiendo el guía y la siguiente vez con guía privado. Gran diferencia. El lugar por si mismo vale el viaje, las incomodidad de los horarios para tomar el avión a Cusco. Ya que no hay vuelos directos de México hacia allá. Por fuerza tienes que hacer escala en Lima. Vale la pena después de haber pagado un boleto de tren tan caro de Oyataitambo hacia Aguascalientes. Haber pagado una noche de habitación en ese lugar. Todo eso valió la pena. La subida en el bus hacia el lugar del Machupicchu simplemente fascinante. Las vistas desde dentro de los vestigios son maravillosas. El pensar como construyeron toda esa ciudadela, es algo que te deja asombrado. En fin, creo que es uno de los lugares que me he quedado mas que asombrado.. No se lo pierdan...

text_clean: Fuimos a la salida de un curso el día domingo, y esta fue mi segunda vez asistiendo. Sin embargo, por segunda vez consecutiva noté los mismos detalles que deberían mejorar. Los recorridos son demasiado planos y se vuelven algo monótonos, además de que los grupos son muy grandes, lo que hace que no todos los visitantes presten atención o se sientan incluidos. En mi caso, la señorita que guiaba avanzaba muy rápido y parecía dirigirse solo a un grupo reducido de personas, dejando de lado a los demás, sin atender las dudas que teníamos. También avanzaba a su propio ritmo, sin esperar que el resto del grupo se reagrupe, lo cual me pareció poco considerado. Fuera de esos puntos, el lugar en sí está muy bien cuidado. Se nota el cariño y el buen manejo que tienen, ya que todo se ve limpio, ordenado y en excelente estado. Aunque todo está muy bien conservado, hay ciertas partes como la zona final del recorrido, en la parte más alta de la huaca endonde se sienten algunos tramos un poco peligrosos, sobre todo al descender, ya que hay curvas pronunciadas sin barandas o elementos de seguridad que podrían evitar accidentes. En general, es un lugar valioso y bien preservado, pero el servicio de guiado y la seguridad en algunos tramos podrían mejorar mucho la experiencia del visitante.

text_clean: Un lugar tan hermoso, extrañe disfrutar y admirar el espectáculo de las piletas, la música clásica con holograma del circuito de aguas, uno se relajaba! Pero me dió pena visitar este lugar y encontrar cantidad de ambulantes originando desorden, ver espacios llenos de basura, la gente comiendo y caminando y pisando los jardines, maltratando el pasto. Esquivar los 3 trenes que lo único que hacen es tocar la bocina y arremeter con todo. Quien fue el genio que autorizó los trenes? Porque no ordenan a los ambulantes, que vendan, limpien y cuiden los espacios, (venden un aparato luminoso que vuela y luego le cae a cualquier persona) Este desorden va a originar un accidente. Este circuito de aguas, ganó un premio a nivel internacional, por lo que era! Nosotros mismos no cuidamos lo bueno que tenemos, nos falta educación, cultura, respeto.

text_clean: We spent an afternoon and the better part of the following day at MP. The two days we were there the clouds were rolling over the mountain tops and created an incredible, life experience type of view. I recommend getting a guide to best understand what you are viewing and the history. This place is not easy to get to including the long lines to get the bus ride from the valley to the entrance near the top of the mountain (only way to get there). Crowds are an issue both in MP and getting up there. We stayed at the small lodge right by the entrance which helped greatly in our enjoyment due to missing some of the crowds and avoiding two bus rides. Expensive and hard to get a room but worth it if your budget allows. The views are incredible, breath taking, etc... The place defies imagination as to how the Incas were able to construct it.

text_clean: La entrada cuesta 4 soles, cuándo fui sólo funcionaba el primer parque que está en Petit Thouars, no funcionaba el de la Av Arequipa porque decían estaba en mantenimiento, el parque está muy bonito y el espectáculo de luces empieza 6:30 pm al parecer, al entrar me pareció extraño ver tantas señoras con muchas bolsas pensé venían de viaje de la sierra, pero casi a las 6 pm empezaron a sacar su mercadería de las bolsas para vender dentro del parque, así perjudican a las personas que alquilan dentro del parque un local y venden, desde la boletería y el personal del parque se hacen de la vista gorda y dejan pasar ambulantes y eso le quita el orden, los churros dentro del parque cuestan 3 soles y los que venden los ambulantes dentro cuesta 1 sol y es pasado y feo. Claro que el choripán que venden dentro del parque cuesta 5 soles y es horrible no lo compren, pero hay otros negocios y los ambulantes perjudican esos negocios.Lo demás el circuito de las aguas y limpieza están muy bien. Le pregunté a los vigilantes por qué dejan entrar ambulantes y no sabían que responderme.

text_clean: There is a lot of hype surrounding Machu Picchu but it is all worth it. See the list below for a list of pros and cons. Note: I took the bus up and it was quite hot that day (25+ degrees Celsius.) I also went to view the Inca Bridge. Pros: - Breathtaking views - Llama and Alpaca for tourist photos - Inca Bridge was beautiful - pictures turn out gorgeous no matter what phone / camera you use to take them on - Amazing history (try to go with a guide who can explain it) Cons: - Fairly crowded, you have to wait for people to come down as there are long lines - Lots of steps (if you have bad knees, make sure you have some sort of reinforcement to help) - Not 100% suitable for children (unless you don't mind carrying them throughout all of Machu Picchu) - Morning ticket only allows you to stay for 5 hours Try to go as soon as possible, as many tour guides say that this specific site may not be open in a couple more years!

label: neutro

text_clean: Es un buen lugar para conocer la Lima pre incaica, la huaca está en un excelente estado de conservación, tiene un tour que demora 1 hora aproximadamente, y hay que ir dispuestos a ensuciarse los zapatos, pues todo el camino de la huaca es tierra. Normalmente en los tours se llega a subir hasta la parte superior de la huaca y creo que eso es el principal atractivo, pero otras veces suspenden el ingreso, me pasó dos veces hace unos años. Tiene un restaurante también pero parece aislado del complejo arqueológico, pues los precios ahí son exorbitantes y debe hacerse reserva previa para ingresar.

text_clean: Este lugar del centro de Lima Perú es un lugar muy maravilloso, y donde se respira parte de la historia colonial del Perú, sus edificaciones y su bella decoración en todo su esplendor. me parece que la plaza de armas de Lima con el grna palacio presidencial y rodeada de tantos hermosos balcones son un punto indiscutible para una visita, ademas hay restaurantes y centros comerciales a los alrededores que pueden sacar de un apuro a cualquier viajero.. recomendado. En su arrededor la arquitectura es nuy espectacular plaza es digna de un birreinato, sencillamente fue una tarde de disfrutar todos los espacios que la rodean y disfrutar un rico almuerzo en uno de los restaurantes mas emblematicos de la ciudad ubicado diagonal a la casa de la literatura de Peru. Indicar que la plaza Mayor, es hermosa por su arquitectura colonial,se puede observar el cambio de guardia en la mañana..Es el corazón de Lima,es un espacio seguro para caminar y punto de partida para la caminata por el centro histórico.A su alrededor se encuentra el palacio de gobierno,la catedral de lima,la iglesia del sagrario,el palacio arzobispal,el palacio municipal y el club de la unión.SE encuentra circundada por Jirón Junín,Jirón de la unión,Jirón de Huallaga y Jirón Carabaya.Cerca se encuentran restaurantes de comida típica y el malecón artesanal. les aconsejo apartarse una mañanita o tarde para ir a la plaza. Si ya están en Lima háganse una escapadita. Es tradicional, linda pintoresca y bien cuidada. Me recomendaron andar con cuidado con los objetos personales por esa zona yo no tuve inconvenientes, pero nunca está demás decirlo.. Enfrente hay una iglesia, la municipalidad, y es la típica foto en la plaza de Lima.. Vale la pena ir.. En la cercanía hay locales comerciales, no del estilo de artículos regionales.

text_clean: It’s a magical place but here are the tips : you need to get there early if you want to feel the magic. Later on thousands of tourists will pour. -get to the gate at the bottom around 4 am and you should be first in the queue -once it opens at 5am run up. There are plenty of stone steps and it takes an average tourist between 1h-2h but if you re fit you can do it in 30 minutes. this way you beat all the tourist buses and you are first at the entrance to Machu Picchu at the top. Then you just walk in and you have the whole place to yourself for 30 minutes ! -third advice is be careful when you are in the Machu Picchu not to go down ( sightseeing it )too early . It’s all one way traffic and local guards will not let you come back up to for example take better pictures because fog cleared. -their is no time limit stay at the top al long as you like just to make sure you soak the place up -before going down to see the chambers /ruins go up to puerta del sol for a stunning view ! You won’t be able to do it if you come down ...

text_clean: Machu Picchu Mountain used to be free to hike (with your day-pass to the park), but now comes at an additional charge to your ticket. I'm from Alaska, and no stranger to hiking mountains, 2-3 thousand feet of elevation in an afternoon is no problem for me, so I didn't expect issues on this trail. But hiking at elevation is a different ball-game, it was a completely geuling trek. And due to the new ticketing system, you have to be out of the park by noon (only the morning ticket holders can buy the mountain trail pass) so there's really a rush to get up and down in time to see some of the rest of the park before you have to leave. The trail is poorly managed and cared for with trash everywhere, and even a few spots that have become informal lavatories, and have not been cleaned up in some time. And that isn't even to mention the insanity that is getting to the park in the first place. If you read on the official website not to bring food, plastic water bottles, or trekking polls, forget all that; nobody on the park staff cares about any of the rules, and following them made the hike much more difficult than it would otherwise have had to be, especially with a bad knee that hurts going downhill, I would have loved to have my trekking polls with me, well, plenty of people ignored the rules and were let right in with all their snacks, extra water, and polls. It's not all bad, there are several bluffs and overlooks on the way up that offer absolutely incredible views of the Machu Picchu site, and surrounding valley, some were even better than the view from the peak. And you're hiking on original Inca laid stone, which is an experience in itself. There are a few sections with sheer drops from with the trail maybe 2 feet wide amd there are no guard or hand rails, that's a little hair-raising, but most of the trail is 5 or 6 feet wide. At the top is a small roofless wooden structure with a few benches for resting (the only ones on the entire hike), and a sign with the peak's elevation. That's it. No signage about what the Inca used the top of the mountain for (it's their trail after all), or a map showing the location of other sites visible from the peak and how they related to each other, or anything at all informative, and although you're now required to enter the park with a guide, they don't go up the mountain with you, so no help there. All in all, I don't reccomend the mountain trail unless you're staying at the Belmond and already planning on more than one day in the park. The logistics of getting their are too crazy, and a half-day in the park too expensive to spend it on one hiking trail, the rest of the park is far more interesting anyway.

text_clean: Increíble lugar, aunque sea una vez en la vida debemos visitar! Para visitar MP, sacar las entradas con varios días de anticipación, ahora se hace todo desde la web de Machu Picchu, se puede pagar con tarjeta o en las oficinas que están cerca de la plaza Regocijo. Atención con el horario, porque solo se puede entrar en el horario especifico. Aun así, si te toca el último turno y no te conviene, puedes entrar hasta las 7:25 am siempre y cuando vayas con un guía. El guía cobra 20 soles por persona o 100 por un grupo familiar. Ya estando allí, habrá varios guardianes que verán que no se saquen el tapabocas en ningún momento. Lo niños tienen más tolerancia en ese aspecto. Está todo muy bien cuidado y señalizado. El detalle que malogra todo el paseo es el tren local, que como turistas nacionales tenemos la opción de tomar . Sin embargo, la tensión por conseguir un boleto es terrible, no debería ser así ya que al final todos viajan, la caseta de serenazgo te da un turno y con eso deberías evitar colas. Hay mucha especulación y también eso da pie para que algunos taxistas te quieran llevar a otras estaciones tratando de sacar ventaja e incluso engañar a los turistas. Tengan cuidado con eso. Fuera de eso, todo excelente 👍

text_clean: Vista 2019 un lugar hermoso y amplio pero algo abandonado cuando fuimos estaban haciendo reparaciones consultamos y nos dijieron que esas reparaciones estaban así hace años sin terminar. Desde la entrada puedes subir a la ciudadela a pie o a caballo. En la misma ciudadela todo es ordenando y limpio hay tachos por todas partes pero siempre hay una que otra persona que no entiende y ensucia igual. Los únicos baños están en la entrada, así que te recomiendo ir al baño antes de comenzar la caminata
