text_clean: Machu Picchu es considerada al mismo tiempo una obra maestra de la arquitectura y la ingeniería.5 Sus peculiares características arquitectónicas y paisajísticas, y el velo de misterio que ha tejido a su alrededor buena parte de la literatura publicada sobre el sitio, lo han convertido en uno de los destinos turísticos más populares del planeta. Machu Picchu está en la Lista del Patrimonio de la humanidad de la Unesco desde 1983, como parte de todo un conjunto cultural y ecológico conocido bajo la denominación Santuario histórico de Machu Picchu. Las ruinas incas se encuentran a medio camino entre las cimas de ambas montañas, a 450 metros de altura por encima del nivel del valle y a 2.438 metros sobre el nivel del mar. La superficie edificada es aproximadamente de 530 metros de largo por 200 de ancho, contando con 172 edificios en su área urbana. Las ruinas, propiamente dichas, están dentro de un territorio intangible del Sistema Nacional de Áreas Naturales Protegidas por el Estado (SINANPE),8 llamado Santuario Histórico de Machu Picchu, que se extiende sobre una superficie de 32.592 hectáreas, (80.535 acres o 325,92 km²) de la cuenca del río Vilcanota-Urubamba (el Willka mayu o "río sagrado" de los incas). El Santuario Histórico protege una serie de especies biológicas en peligro de extinción y varios establecimientos incas, entre los cuales Machu Picchu es considerado principal. Las primeras referencias directas sobre visitantes de las ruinas de Machu Picchu indican que Agustín Lizárraga, un arrendatario de tierras cusqueño, llegó al sitio el 14 de julio de 1902 guiando a los también cusqueños Gabino Sánchez, Enrique Palma y Justo Ochoa. Los visitantes dejaron un graffiti con sus nombres en uno de los muros del Templo de las Tres Ventanas que fue posteriormente verificado por varias personas. Existen informaciones que sugieren que Lizárraga ya había visitado Machu Picchu en compañía de Luis Béjar en 1894.48 Lizárraga les mostraba las construcciones a los "visitantes", aunque la naturaleza de sus actividades no ha sido hasta hoy investigada. Bingham quedó muy impresionado por lo que vio y gestionó los auspicios de la Universidad de Yale, la National Geographic Society y el gobierno peruano para iniciar de inmediato el estudio científico del sitio. Así, con el ingeniero Ellwood Erdis, el osteólogo George Eaton, la participación directa de Toribio Recharte y Anacleto Álvarez y un grupo de anónimos trabajadores de la zona, Bingham dirigió trabajos arqueológicos en Machu Picchu en 1912 hasta 1915 período en el que se despejó la maleza y se excavaron tumbas incas en los extramuros de la ciudad. La "vida pública" de Machu Picchu empieza en 1913 con la publicación de todo ello en un artículo en la revista de la National Geographic. Si bien es claro que Bingham no descubre Machu Picchu en el sentido estricto de la palabra (nadie lo hizo dado que nunca se "perdió" realmente), es indudable que tuvo el mérito de ser la primera persona en reconocer la importancia de las ruinas, estudiándolas con un equipo multidisciplinario y divulgando sus hallazgos. Ello pese a que los criterios arqueológicos empleados no fueran los más adecuados desde la perspectiva actual, y pese, también, a la polémica que hasta hoy envuelve la más que irregular salida del país del material arqueológico excavado (que consta de al menos unas 46.332 piezas) y que hasta el 2009 no ha sido devuelto al gobierno peruano. El área edificada en Machu Picchu es de 530 metros de largo por 200 de ancho e incluye al menos 172 recintos. El complejo está claramente dividido en dos grandes zonas: la zona agrícola, formada por conjuntos de terrazas de cultivo, que se encuentra al sur; y la zona urbana, que es, por supuesto, aquella donde vivieron sus ocupantes y donde se desarrollaron las principales actividades civiles y religiosas. Ambas zonas están separadas por un muro, un foso y una escalinata, elementos que corren paralelos por la cuesta este de la montaña

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).

text_clean: Kuélap es un lugar histórico, una antigua cuidadela de la cultura Chachapoyas. Me gusta como conservan el lugar y te exigen respetarlo. Atención a quienes la visiten: está prohibido salir de los senderos señalados y tocar las paredes de piedra. Los lugareños y el Ministerio de Cultura han hecho un excelente trabajo por mantenerlo en buenas condiciones y debemos comportarnos como con cualquier otro legado de nuestros antepasados. Para llegar a la fortaleza puedes ir a pie en un recorrido de 9 km o usar el teleférico (30 min). Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.

Ya en el lugar se paga una entrada de 20 soles por adultos y 10 para niños o preferenciales (estudiantes, docentes, adultos mayores). Los extrajeros pagan más. También debes tener en cuenta que si vas el primer domingo del primer mes es gratis. Por cierto, después de eso tienes que caminar un corto tramo o puedes alquilar un caballo. El camino está arreglado y señalizado. Arriba encontrarán guías, si deseas contratar uno. Y si entras sin guía, de todas formas debes seguir la ruta marcada. La entrada principal está en mantenimiento desde hace un año (es difícil recuperarlo). Inmediatamente después de pagar la entrada hay servicios higiénicos, un café, un vivero de orquídeas, un museo, un mirador y tiendas de souvenirs. Realmente no recomiendo comprar cosas o comida allá porque todo es excesivamente caro.
label: negativo

text_clean: 500+ year old ruins of Incan imperial retreat. Stunning views, amazing history, good workout. Bring bug spray (mites/gnats will devour your legs). Walking sticks are helpful. Not wheelchair accessible, and extremely difficult for people with mobility challenges. Staff are friendly and helpful. One of the most photogenic places on earth. Visited on 1st Oct. Sunny at 9am, overcast at 10am, raining at 11am, and sunny again by noon. Wasn't too terribly crowded, but everyone moves at their own pace, so sometimes you pass someone, other times you get passed. Llamas at the top of Circuit 2... not aggressive, will let you pet them if you take your time. Food and toilets available at the trailhead. No hired guides necessary. Queues for the bus up and back down are inconceivably long! Bring bottled water!

text_clean: It's a beautiful place! It's a place where you can sit and appreciate the view and the movimentation. It's the main square with lot of bars, restaurants, stores, gift shops surrounding. It's the most secure place because is where the Town Hall and I guess the parlament is placed. The square is a World Heritage Site, declared by UNESCO, and so then is forbidden to protest and make political acts. All this acts can be done in another square very closed to the Main Square. Thats is why the square is so clean as well. At midday you can see the Changing of the Guards but it's not something big. It's just a cool formality. I enjoyed been there because is a historical place too. Many important events happened there and during the Football World Cup the watched the matches there. It's a place that you should visit!

text_clean: Visited on 4/24/24, which was my birthday, and it was one of the most epic experiences of my life. It was so amazing that we decided to go back the following day as well. There’s little that hasn’t already been said about this location and the history is well known so I will spare that part of my review but honestly, this is a destination that everyone should visit at least once in their life. I saw very young people all the way up to people in their 90s so it really is possible for anyone except those with mobility or health issues. The mystery and beauty of Machu Picchu are reasons enough to visit, but just being there and walking the grounds you really feel connected to history and the views are absolutely breathtaking. Our first day we went with a guide and the second time we went by ourselves. Both were great experiences. You can walk up to the entrance of Machu Picchu but I would suggest taking a bus because it will take multiple hours to get up there. You can also take the Inka Trail (3 nights and 4 days) and hike down into Machu Picchu so there are multiple different methods of getting there. We decided to stay in Aguas Calientes for a few nights so we took the bus to and from each visit. Our first day we went with a guide who took us around but the second day we decided to choose Circuit 2 which many have said is the best route. It is definitely the most comprehensive and you get to see most of the site this way. We also added the Inka Bridge which was closed when we visited, but we were able to get very close to it. It’s an extra half mile climb once you reach the top of Machu Picchu but honestly it’s pretty easy, however, I would avoid it if you are afraid of heights because the pathway there is very narrow and the drop off to the side is hundreds, if not over 1000, feet to the bottom. I am still in awe of everything I saw and it really left me with an immense appreciation and respect for the Peruvian people and what the Inca accomplished. I could not recommend this site enough and feel that it definitely deserves the UNESCO world heritage site badge as well as being one of the new Seven Wonders of the World.

text_clean: Stunning site, but plan ahead Machu Picchu is breathtaking and absolutely worth the trip. However, the logistics can be tricky. We waited a frustrating 18 hours for tickets because we didn’t book in advance—big mistake! Entrance fees are fair, but additional costs like the ~$24 round-trip bus and extra charges for guides quickly add up. And of course you need to calculate a round-trip train+bus tickets from Cusco, too. Despite the crowds and commercialization, this place is pure magic and it’s worth every step. If possible, go later in the day when the crowds thin and the light hits just right. Machu Picchu will stay with you long after you’ve left—but only if you avoid the ticket fiasco, so go prepared and you’ll have the time of your life! TIPS: - Book tickets months ahead and hire a guide at the entrance - Take the bus from Aguas Calientes unless you’re up for a steep 2-hour hike. - Circuit 2 offers the best views and flexibility. - Bring water, sunscreen, and a camera, you’ll need all three.

Tráfico , suciedad en las calles tugurizadas, vendedores ambulantes cada medio metro . Y no , no se ve bonito . Una lástima pues el centro de Lima no es únicamente su plaza de Armas , veremos si algún alcalde logra ordenar más la ciudad .

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Pero vaya que lugar tan increíble, nunca antes lo había visitado y siempre veía y escuchaba hablar del museo, por lo que siempre estaba en mi lista de lugares del que tenía que visitar y ya lo hice pero a pesar de ello, volvería a visitarlo una vez más; porque tiene muchos detalles e historia por aprender y un día no basta. text_clean: Nunca habia ido a la Reserva y me pareció muy lindo todo. Asistí con unos sobrinos argentinos de 12 y 9 años, al explicarles lo q era una reserva comenzaron a recolectar vasitos, envolturas y más. Lamentablemente mi sobrina piso algo punzante de metal q gracias a Dios no se lo clavó. Fuí a hacerle saber a los guardias y me explicaron q todos los dias limpian las playas pero q la gente no colabora eso es muy triste. Me dijeron q vaya al museo q alli me darian unos polos por colaborar con la reserva, los chicos entusiasmados quisieron ir pero lamentablemente ya no tenian polos y les regalaron un librito para pintar y desarrollar unos laberintos, les dieron también esas sintas donde se cuela el alcohol, mascarillas y un toma todo de metal muy lindo. Todo rotulado con nombre de la reserva. Felices nos retiramos del museo q por cierto es muy interesante. Gracias por alentar a los niños y motivarlos a cuidar el medio ambiente!!

text_clean: Arriving here at 6 am and seeing the fog slowly lift off the citadel and the surrounding mountains was the highlight of our entire trip. Even with all the hype, the place was bigger and better than what I expected. We spent the first few hours touring the citadel and then did the Wayna Picchu hike. We were only able to get out once to use the restrooms which was a little stressful, because you do want to stay hydrated while hiking and eventually need to pee and the bathrooms are located outside the exit which is all the way across the citadel from WP entrance. We also could have done with a better guide, we may have not had good luck with ours. He repeated himself several times and did not seem to give us many details. Our ollantaytambo tour guide did a better job and we were able to understand the symbolism from his descriptions to us than our MP guide which was a little disappointing.

text_clean: Amazing place with mysterious vibes! Unforgettable experience! Will go there all the time I am in Peru! Some suggestions: Buy ticket as early as you can! I bought it one month earlier and it was almost ran out. Also suggest to take circuit 2, it covers most of the fabulous places. All the trails are circular, so take your time any place you like, you cannot go backwards. Be on time, they check entrance time and take into account the queues before entrance. If you stay in Aguas Calientes it's better to get there as early as you can, there are queues for the bus also. My entrance time was at 7 a.m., I was on bus station at 5:45 a.m. and got the third bus.

text_clean: Es una plaza bonita, amplia pero le falta más zonas verdes, está rodeada por la Casa de Gobierno, el Arzobispado, la Catedral, Municipalidad de Lima y negocios todos hermosas edificaciones antiguas y bien cuidadas, lamentablemente no se puede apreciar hacia más de un año que casi se ma tiene cerrada la policía no deja que entren según dice que por seguridad y eso impide el turismo y los negocios cercanos de artesanías sufren las consecuencias. Hay un caballo con una carreta que pasean al rededor no se cuánto cobran, cerca está la Iglesia de San Francisco donde se puede hacer un recorrido pagado por las catacumbas, también está cerca la Alameda Chabuca Granda un lugar que fue rescatado de la delincuencia, ahora encuentras bastantes carritos de venta de comida y postres típicos muy ricos y de precio cómodos también puedes ver grupos de música, baile y si es de tu gusto los cómicos ambulantes. Hay vigilancia pero de todas maneras hay que tener cuidado muy visitado por turistas en un solo lugar encuentras gran variedad de la gastronomía peruana sobre todo limeña. No recomiendo ir fin de semana hay muchísima gente.

text_clean: ¡¿Que más se puede decir de este lugar maravilloso?! :D Ver la ciudad inca de Machupicchu es sin duda un imperdible en tu viaje por Perú. Es importante que compres tu ticket de entrada con meses de anticipación, sobre todo si quieres subir al Huaynapicchu. Si solo quieres visitar la ciudadela, entonces puedes comprar la entrada con unos cuantos días de antes. Te recomendamos entrar en el horario de las 6am ya que a esa hora la mayoría de los visitantes están haciendo fotos y la ciudadela está vacía. Ademas, con un poco de suerte podrás ver pequeñas nubes de neblina que harán mejor tus fotos. Para subir puedes hacerlo a pie o en bus. Es recomendable pagar el bus de ida y bajar las escalinatas de regreso. Así evitarás llegar cansado a Machupicchu. No olvides llevar suficiente agua, pequeños snack, gorra y protector solar. (Todo en una pequeña mochila para que puedas guardar tu basura). En teoría la entrada con guía es obligatoria, sin embargo puedes optar entrar por tu cuenta, hacer todas las fotos que quieras y cuando estés listo unirte a algún grupo pidiendo permiso al guía y por supuesto dando tu respectivo pago. Recuerda que los baños solo están a la entrada al igual que el servicio de guarda equipajes. Al salir no te olvides de poner tu sello en el pasaporte como recuerdo de tu visita a Machupicchu ;)

text_clean: Unfortunately had 2 days (visiting afternoon then morning on consecutive days) when weather was not good. 1st day in particular very wet (rain) and no "long views" due to heavy mist. Second day was better in terms of less rain/mist but being Sunday(?) it was VERY busy. All that said, this is truly a 'magical' space and is best visited (at least) twice, once with a guide but the 2nd time without thereby allowing the opportunity to spend longer at points of 'personal' interest AND as in my case, being able to explore parts that are pointed out by guides but not visited, due to time constraints. Beware the bus up in the morning will entail long queues (although buses are frequent) and it's similar in the afternoon coming down. Be prepared with all relevant documentation to avoid being stopped from boarding (bus) or entering site. Also be aware that movement around the site tends to be 'one way' and enforced by "guards" with whistles!

text_clean: This is a summary of an August visit to Apu Machu Picchu (towards the end of peak season). If you’ve spent at least two days in Cusco, a night in Aguas Calientes, and stayed well hydrated, altitude should not be a problem. I recommend arriving in Aguas Calientes the afternoon before and overnighting—it will make life easier. We did two routes in a single day: the 7 am Huayna Picchu (Circuit 3A) and the 3 pm Classic Route (Circuit 2A). For me, these were the perfect combination to get a comprehensive experience of Machu Picchu, both as a visitor and a photographer. (I would not suggest squeezing in three routes in one day.) Route 2A has the classic postcard views of Machu Picchu with a walk through the main complex. If you can only do one route, do 2A. Route 3A gives you a slightly different perspective from a lower elevation, with the bonus of hiking Huayna Picchu for stunning birdseye views. Both are doable in one day if you plan carefully. For the 7 am 3A Huayna Picchu entry, we lined up for the bus at 6 am in Aguas Calientes and got through the main gate around 7:30. The bus ride itself is 25–30 minutes, but there is also a line at the entrance to factor in. Morning entry is wonderful for photography, with some sun and cloud play. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu, I recommend taking photos on your way up because the lighting on the return is harsher. From the main entrance it took us about 40–45 minutes to reach the Huayna Picchu checkpoint, navigating crowds, guides, and taking photos. The hike itself is challenging but not as dangerous as people make it out to be. It is essentially a continuous staircase with steep grades. The final notorious section known as “Staircase of Death" is not "deadly". Just take it slow and do what you've been doing. Dry conditions are fine but wet weather would make it riskier. At a moderate pace, expect 50-60 mins to reach the top. With frequent stops, around 75 mins. ~45 mins descent. After a quick lunch in Aguas Calientes, we got in line for the bus at 1:30 pm and entered exactly at 3 for the 3 pm 2A classic route. This circuit is not physically demanding beyond walking, and at a relaxed pace it takes around 1.5 to 2 hours. The famous photo terraces are worth waiting for, but there are also excellent viewpoints further down the route with fewer people. The later you stay, the better the lighting and the cleaner your photos. Even with crowds, the photos comes out nice because the site is expansive. We also met a few llamas who were happy to pose, as long as treated respectfully. Do you need a guide? Not necessarily. With some research—or even AI—you can learn as you go and move at your own pace. Guides can add context but they also tend to block paths for their groups, which can be frustrating. The downside of a group is less flexibility to linger in places that speak to you personally. All circuits are one way, so take photos when you see a good spot—you will not be able to loop back. Weather is dependent on cloud cover. If sunny, it'll be warm. If overcast, it'll be chilly. Facilities include restrooms at the entrance (2 soles), luggage storage (5 soles), and a small snack bar, though eating in Aguas Calientes is better. If you book back-to-back slots (like 7 am and 11 am), do not exit the premises in between or you’ll waste time in lines again. Tickets need to be booked on the official government website using your passport details. The process can seem confusing but is fairly straightforward—switching the page back to Spanish often solved website glitches for me. Book at least 2.5-3 months in advance. This is not a place to “wing it.” Each ticket requires passport information (tour operators might not have special last-moment access.) You must carry your physical passport at all times as it is checked against both bus and site tickets. All tickets are digital PDFs with QR codes, which scanned fine from our phones. Overall, we had an incredible visit! We took the 7 pm train back to Cusco and had arranged a late hotel checkout for a small fee for added convenience.

text_clean: Ancient sacred city in the clouds! The 600 hundred year old architecture and stone work is amazing! Walk on one of the ancient Inca trails and you will come across the famous Inca Bridge. To get the best experience, I recommend a tour guide from a reputable company to appreciate the knowledge and history of Machu Picchu. Be prepared to wait in long cues.You are not alone on the mountain. There are thousands of tourists pounding the walk ways. And sometimes it is a slow walk as the narrow paths are blocked by people taking selfies or that iconic photo. There are no toilets or bathrooms on the site. These are at the gate entrance only. Time is also limited, you can go either to the morning or afternoon session. Feel safe, as there are security discreetly watching. Some areas are restricted with no touch or no entry signs, due to tourists having silly accidents, and also others causing damage to structures. You will see archaeologists at work and maintenance people too. The mountain is accessible to people who have some mobility. Even people with walking sticks and elderly were able to experience the mountain. Remember to take your passport to get the famous Machu Picchu stamp. I would love to go to Machu Picchu again one day.

text_clean: El museo en cuanto a objetos y diseño es un 10/10. PERO llegué 430pm, 1era vez que visito el museo, cuando me vendieron las entradas, nunca me dijeron q la hora de cierre estaba próxima. Después cuando estaba dentro disfrutando del museo, alguien del museo me apuró, me dijo "en 1 hr se ve todo, ajustando 45min". Ir a un museo es una experiencia, sobre todo un museo con tantos detalles como este. No pude leer ni admirar el 50% de los objetos. Cada objeto tiene un texto bastante detallado además de ser obras muy bien trabajadas, obras q uno necesita procesar con tiempo, admirar. Soy de Lima, no sé cuando regrese, lamentablemente no tuve una buena experiencia. Sí antes de venderme la entrada me hubieran dicho "señor estamos próximos a cerrar, tome en cuenta q es probable que el tiempo no le sea suficiente" ya sabría a lo q me estaba lanzando. Sí van a ir, calculen 2 hrs para admirar todo, para procesar el arte de cada obra y no ir al museo solo por entrar y ya.

text_clean: It is one of those Once in a Lifetime kind of experiences, especially if you are traveling from Asia, it is so far to get to and it was almost 3 days of traveling before we actually set foot on Machu Picchu! Most people likely to fly into Cusco and somehow then find their way either by car or train to Aguas Calientes which is the town at the foothill of Machu Picchu. In fact it is also where most people will spend a night if you are not staying at the one and only hotel at the top, which is the Belmond Sanctuary. From Aguas Caliente, across the bridge from the train station and there’s where you will catch a bus up to Machu Picchu. The bus ride is only a short 15min through the winding road uphill or it is possible to hike up which may take you an hour. The current (as of June 18) entrance rule to the Machu Picchu is that there are 2 sessions a day from 6am-12pm and again 12pm-5:30pm. With each entrance ticket, you will get access to the park twice (in and out) as there are no toilet and food service inside the park, you will have to come out and go back in. Strongly recommend that you bring snacks and water as even outside the park, your food options are very limited with extremely long lines. The morning session is significantly more crowded than the afternoon. Since the Incan worship the sun, many visitors like to witness sunrise over Machu Picchu. In fact, you don't really get to see Sunrise as it is blocked by the surrounding mountains, but what you will see (on a clear day) is when the sunlight hits the ancient ruin, which is still a sight to behold. However, if you were only to do 1 entry (we did both the morning and afternoon), I would recommend the afternoon session as the site cleared out by 3pm and you almost have the entire place to yourself. It is then peaceful and quiet. Not only the morning session is filled with people who tries to get into the park for the sunrise, it s also when most Inca Trail hikers enter the park from the other end (through Sun Gate). So the place is rather noisy and crowded. Morning session has other disadvantage, since the bus service starts at 5:30am, there is a long line trying to get into the park before 6am official open time and by 6:30am, there isn't a line anymore. Another problem the morning session give you is that trying to catch a bus back down to Aguas Calientes right around noon (when the morning session ends) the line for the bus downhill can be more than an hour wait.

text_clean: This is one of the spectacular places in the world to visit. It is just as fascinating as you can imagine. I have been wanting to go here since I was a child, and I was not disappointed. Tips for travel: - Buy your tickets in advance. This can be done online. I bought mine from a company on trip advisor which included a hike up Huayna Picchu. It was a little more pricey than buying direct from the Peru tourism website, but I had the confidence of good customer service with people on the ground who could act as a liaison to ensure that I got my tickets. Print them out and bring it with you. - **BRING YOUR PASSPORT** - I recommend taking Peru Rail’s Vistadome train to Aguas Calientes. - Bus tickets up the mountain can be purchased on the spot in Aguas Calientes for $25 round-trip. No need to buy in advance. Purchasing can take 10 minutes, depending on the line. Cash only. Ticket office is located one block from the bus pick up spot. - Bring a poncho, umbrellas are not allowed. - If you are not hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend two hours in the citadel. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend a total of four hours, and be sure to bring water and hiking poles. I cannot emphasize this enough. - The afternoon is less crowded. I would recommend going at that time if your trip schedule allows it. - Be sure to go up to the Sun Gate when you first enter. - All circuits through the citadel are one way. It would be helpful if you plan in advance on which circuit you would like to take. - Bring some snacks, bottles of water, and sunscreen. - There is a hotel, restaurant, baggage storage, and restrooms outside of the entrance. Keep this in mind as you plan. The restroom cost a small fee, so keep coins with you. - And finally, take lots of photos.

text_clean: The bucket-list trip to the Incan ruins (Machu Picchu especially) left my legs sore and my heart full. We were literally on the mountains and in the clouds at the same time- as the clouds passed, you could feel the temp drop a bit and then go back up as they moved out. Note that the whole place is a one way and the staff security does not let you back track if you miss something so take your sweeeeet time and make it to the top lookouts before you realize you missed them. Slow and steady. Staff tour guides are available but not a necessity; interest based.

text_clean: There are no words to accurately describe the majesty of this city in the clouds. As you enter into the city, you feel transported to a new world especially in the morning before the fog has faded. We spent two days at the city. On the first, we hiked Waynapicchu. It is a difficult climb especially at fairly high elevation but you can take your time. If you are able, you must do this hike. It was the highlight of our Peru trip. Sadly, some of the sites were closed for renovation including the Moon Temple. On the second, we explored the city with a tour guide. We booked ours through Adios Adventure Travel and they were great though a little pricy. Even when exploring the city, prepare for a lot of walking and stairs. Llamas (and a few alpacas) wander aroud freely. Make sure you use the restroom prior to entering the city. There is no in and out privilege. You probably should take the bus from Aguas Calientes to Macchu Picchu especially if you expect the walk around Macchu Picchu to be strenous and tiring already. We walked down the mountain hoping to see an Andea bear. It was still beautiful but not a crucial hike. Make sure you purchase your tickets for Macchu Picchu in advance. There are a very limited amount of tickets sold at the gate hence the protests. We were able to avoid waiting in long lines and have security knowing our spot was booked by buying tickets several weeks prior.

It is a difficult climb especially at fairly high elevation but you can take your time. If you are able, you must do this hike. It was the highlight of our Peru trip. Sadly, some of the sites were closed for renovation including the Moon Temple. On the second, we explored the city with a tour guide. We booked ours through Adios Adventure Travel and they were great though a little pricy. Even when exploring the city, prepare for a lot of walking and stairs. Llamas (and a few alpacas) wander aroud freely. Make sure you use the restroom prior to entering the city. There is no in and out privilege. You probably should take the bus from Aguas Calientes to Macchu Picchu especially if you expect the walk around Macchu Picchu to be strenous and tiring already. We walked down the mountain hoping to see an Andea bear. It was still beautiful but not a crucial hike. Make sure you purchase your tickets for Macchu Picchu in advance. There are a very limited amount of tickets sold at the gate hence the protests. We were able to avoid waiting in long lines and have security knowing our spot was booked by buying tickets several weeks prior.

text_clean: The Lima Main Square, known as the Plaza Mayor, is an absolute must-visit for anyone exploring the rich history and culture of Peru. This square is the heartbeat of Lima, surrounded by some of the most iconic and historically significant buildings in the city. The architecture is breathtaking, with the Government Palace, Cathedral of Lima, and Archbishop's Palace dominating the square. The vibrant energy here is infectious, whether it's the local vendors selling traditional Peruvian crafts, the captivating street performances, or the constant buzz of both locals and tourists. This place isn't just a feast for the eyes; it's a journey through time. The stories held within these walls resonate with the heritage of Peru. Visiting this square is like stepping into a living museum, where every corner holds a piece of the city's past. Additionally, the square's central location makes it an excellent starting point for exploring the rest of Lima. You can easily access other attractions, restaurants, and shops from here. However, as it's a bustling tourist spot, it can get quite crowded during peak hours. Regardless, the vibrant atmosphere and the historical significance outweigh the crowds. A visit to Lima isn't complete without immersing yourself in the beauty and history of the Lima Main Square. It's a definite highlight and an essential stop for any traveler. Pro tip: Consider a guided tour to fully grasp the historical importance and stories behind the buildings and the square itself.

text_clean: This is an amazing place! Incredible location in the exuberant ANDES . The tracks going to the point are unforgettable, that's the essence and uniqueness of those evergreen mountains. Rich full of life with wild rivers, dangerous trails and "pacha mama's " fruits of delight😂. The views and the natural phenomena situation make Machu Picchu a memorable place. So international!!! On the other hand stay the construction itself : very modest and elementary for the time ( Incas like all American tribes ) were underdeveloped and it is evident in all their monuments around the continent. Lack of any written documents make the stories sound so funny and absurd! Tour guides are so amateurish that make me LOL! Extremely overpriced but that's the sad story of the mass tourism industry all around the world now!

text_clean: A very beautiful trekking area and unlike anywhere else in Peru. We spent two days trekking this area without a guide or tour, which is totally unnecessary in this canyon. The trails couldn’t be easier to follow and the whole area is perfect for fit hikers but beginner trekkers. Don’t be fooled though, the steep drop into the canyon and painful return climb to the rim are harder than most hikes, and reminiscent of those found in the Grand Canyon. We did the long classic route which descends to Llahuar and loops to San Juan before climbing back up to Cabanaconde. This is typically done over three days but we got to Llahuar by 10:00 on day one so competed the second days hike as well. That day was around 20+ kilometers and tough but rewarding. The next morning, we climbed from San Juan to the rim, which was quite a bit longer and tougher than expected. We had decided to skip San Galle which is on most trek itineraries as it is the most touristy of the villages in the canyon. We stayed at Colibri Lodge for our night inside, which was one of the nicer guesthouse we found in South America. No reservation, just got lucky with a walk in and they had availability for us. The only downside of trekking here are the ridiculously limited bus options. Unless you are coming from or going to Arequipa, there really aren’t any public buses available. There are a handful of tourist buses, but they all make lame stops at expensive restaurants, viewpoints, etc and cost $50-65 USD for Puno or Cusco. We didn’t want to go all the way back to Arequipa since we were headed to Cusco, and managed to find a public bus option to get there without backtracking. It included a few steps and a little adventure, but cost just 50 soles and was actually pretty easy. And let’s face it, you wouldn’t be trekking in Peru if you didn’t like adventure. If you want specific info on the trekking routes and distances, how to do this without a guide, or most importantly all of the transport options for accessing the canyon and then getting to places other than Arequipa, search our name, Career Break Adventures and Colca Canyon or something of that nature. Have fun and enjoy the trek!

I also would have loved to walk around and explore myself. But sadly it's not permitted. Luckily the tour was only 1 hour.

text_clean: Unbelievable in person. Pictures do not do it justice. Here’s some helpful tips as an American tourist on their first trip to Macchu Picchu: 1 Buy your tickets well in advance. There is a huge line at the tourist office in Aguas Calientes for last minute ticket purchases. It’s chaotic, skip the headache and plan ahead. We were out in Aguas Calientes for two nights and both nights the line / crowd was huge. Do your research on the best time to go. We went early October (last week) - the temperature was perfect. Crispy in the early morning then warmed up later on. Sun is strong, wear sunscreen and bug spray. 2 If you can, and are up for the challenge, do the hiking trail up. Not the Inca Trail. There’s a walking path that’s about ~1+ hour (longer if you take breaks). It’s 100% worth the effort for the views. It’s like the stair-master on hard. There are buses that run very frequently up but they’re not for the faint of heart or for impatient people who hate waiting in long lines. The buses fly up the sides of the mountain (no thank you). 3 Get an early ticket. It gets crowded. We had a 9am and it was great. It was crowded when we were leaving about 2 hours later. 4 There’s a snack counter and a formal restaurant. The snack counter is 10/10 with beer, cocktails, desserts, and snacks (chicken empanada is A1). Grab something before you go in as you can’t eat when you’re in there. 5 You cannot get in ahead of your ticket time. They’re strict about this. 6 BRING YOUR PASSPORT 7 Get a guide. There’s no informational signs that describe what you’re looking at. If you don’t have someone describing things you’re just there for the views (even though we left with more questions than answers) 8 Bring soles for the bathroom. 2 soles to enter. 9 Respect the beauty of the site

En la otra espere 30 minutos e igual no me dejaron entrar porque mi otro hermano ya tenía más de 10 años. Cómo hago? Me divido entonces. Su lógica está mal. Yo soy muy comprensiva pero si me mandan de un lado a otro y espero un montón entonces no hay respeto ni flexibilidad. Tenía que cuidar a ambos y no puedo entrar con uno y dejar al otro. Pagando estacionamiento, entradas y comida todo para que mis hermanos terminen llorando porque no entraron juntos. Pésimo.

text_clean: 😉Fue un deseo de visitar a la Montaña Huaynapicchu y ver parte de las ruinas del gran imperio inca, como dicen por ahí la montaña es un reto mas aun cuando esta en épocas de lluvia el camino es difícil y muy peligroso, pero no me importo por que escalaba esa montaña en el día de mi cumpleaños; tuve una gran suerte de que mi agente turística estaba muy pendiente de mi y se las recomendare busque a la señora Linda Illatupa su Whatsapp es 939177232, hagan presente mi nombre para que reciban una rebaja en sus tours los demás que pululan por la plaza te incrementan cuando no conoces los precios. Lo único malo y no es por parte de los agentes turísticos es el problema del tren mi agente en todo momento me decía que ni bien baje del bus de retorno inmediatamente reservar tu boleto del tren si no están pendientes uds. mismo pueden perder el tren y quedarse un día en el pueblo y el hostal mas barato esta 120 soles y la comida ni que decir, el tren algunas veces llega a llevar solo 400 o 500 pasajeros y eso si sacas con tiempo tu ticket de retorno solo hay 3 salidas del tren 2 5 y 9 de la noche, en ese dia que viaje era el numero 160 y mi retorno estaba programado a las 5:00pm y solo dijeron que del 30 al 200 subirán al tren y el resto turno de las 9:00pm de la noche ese mismo día a promediar 9:50pm cae una fuerte lluvia con crecida de rio y hubo derrumbe en esa zona hubo 3 desaparecidos y las demás personas quedaron varados todo esto me entere en la mañana con mi agente de turismo pues el decía que si abordaría en el tren y otras personas que estaban con ella mucha no llegaron en ese día....pero como dice lo vivido nadie me lo quita nada mas hermoso de conocer dicha maravilla👍

text_clean: El lugar está lindo, con atracciones y fuentes para niños y adultos. Lo malo: afuera había fila y una señora que trabajaba ahí salía con un megáfono a regañar a las personas de la fila. Fue muy desagradable escuchar sus gritos, en especial cuando uno se apresta a divertirse. Luego, adentro hay mucho comercio ambulante y te cobran por entrar a otros recintos, incluyendo el baño (es insólito, porque ya te cobran por entrar al parque). Hay un espectáculo de luz, agua y sonido, pero empezó muy tarde, 45 minutos después, y eso hizo que la gente se aburriera y, en general, causará decepción. Ojo: adentro hay un mariposario y dejan las luces encendidas hasta tarde para que las mariposas no se duerman y la gente pueda verlas (previo pago, por supuesto). Eso es generarle estrés a las mariposas por unos soles. No volvería, aunque es entretenido.

text_clean: I totally recommend this restaurant. It's a bit expensive, but it's worth it. It's peruvian food gourmet style, hard to find these presentations of dishes in other restaurants, plus you have the view of the ruins of Huaca Pucllana. All dishes I ordered were so good. I even liked the "fake" rocoto relleno, which is an entree from Arequipa. I usually don't enjoy this entree, but here it was so good! The environment is nice, but now that is summer, I was sweating sitting outside in the terrace, even with a fan next to us (which was uncomfortable), I regret not choosing another table to sit. Other than that, everything was excellent.

text_clean: Machu Picchu fue espectacular! Acá algunas consideraciones para que tu visita sea placentera: - Antes de tu visita a Machu Picchu, compra tus entradas por su web (es la única vía para comprar las entradas) con varias semanas de anticipación, ya que se acaban muy rápido. Si no las compras con tiempo, corres el riesgo de tener que hacer cola en Aguas Calientes y gastar más de lo tenías previsto. - Una vez que llegues a Aguas Calientes, ve a la oficina de venta de Tickets para el bus. El pasaje cuesta 15 dólares ida y vuelta y tienes la opción de pagarlo en soles al tipo de cambio del día. Puedes pagar en efectivo o tarjeta. - Los paraderos de buses a Machu Picchu tienen unos letreros con unos horarios, el cual indican la hora de entrada a Machu Picchu, por lo que tienes que estar 1 hora antes haciendo cola para el bus. - En la entrada a Machu Picchu hay baño a 2 soles y guardarropa a 5 soles. - Te recomiendo que vayas con un guía que te ayudará con tu recorrido. Cobran entre 25 a 30 soles. - Para el ingreso, solo te piden presentar tu DNI en físico y lo cotejan con lo que esta ingresado en su sistema. - Ten en cuenta el tipo de trayecto que compras, ya que cada trayecto tiene un recorrido determinado y no puedes volver si es que te equivocas. - Para el regreso, tienes que irte al paradero de bus. Ahí solo haces cola hasta que se llene el bus y ya no tienes que esperar hasta cierto horario. Eso seria todo. Espero tengan una gran experiencia. Gracias! 🤗

text_clean: Bellísimo, una experiencia que hay que vivir al menos una vez en la vida. Me faltan palabras para poder describir lo maravilloso que es este lugar, por lo mismo, te recomiendo a ojos cerrados que lo visites, para que entiendas de lo que te hablo. Se puede hacer por cuenta propia, o con agencia, con esta última hay que tener especial cuidado, por qué hay algunas que son un tanto desorganizadas, y pueden arruinar tu experiencia. Los precios en promedio rondan los s/350 pp, o $110 dólares en promedio. Recomiendo agencia Pullman, ubicada en el 2do piso del terminal de buses de Cusco, Of. 205. Muy amables, atentos y lo más importante, cumplen a cabalidad con lo que ofrecen. Por cuenta propia, encuentras buses que salen directo dela mismo terminal hacia hidroeléctrica, que es donde comienza la ruta a pie hacia la ciudad de Aguascalientes por las vías del tren. El ticket en tren de hidroeléctrica hacia aguas calientes, cuesta algo de 15dls. Caminando, tardas unas 2:30hrs a llegar al pueblo. El mejor horario para visitar las ruinas, es en el último turno, dado que la cantidad de turistas es la mínima, y se puede recorrer y fotografiar con mayor tranquilidad. Es un tanto restrictiva la visita, pero se entiende, que es por la seguridad de los visitantes.

Creo que con un poco más de empatía y flexibilidad, la experiencia podría ser mucho mejor.

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.

You need to book a bus ticket one week in advance. They run a lot of buses, so even through the lines get long, they move pretty quick. There are no toilets inside Machu Picchu, so use them before you go in. There is a small cafe outside of the gates that are expensive by Peruvian standards but reasonable by European/American standards. The there is often vendors selling cheap Sandwichs and rain panchos below the entrance near the tree line. You can bring small bags and bottles of water into the site, but make sure you take your trash with you. Don't be that tourist...

Al llegar a la fortaleza, supuestamente, tienes que haber sacado un ticket en línea y solo deberían entrar 13 personas a la vez por 45 minutos. En mi turno dejaron entrar como a 30. Bueno, en verdad es a criterio de los de seguridad, por qué habia un trayecto mayor para recorrer pero sin mayor explicación, solo el YO estoy diciendo que no se puede ir por allá, te impiden hacer un mayor recorrido. Si no está listo para recibir turismo, por qué no lo cierran y se dedican a hacer las cosas bien.

text_clean: Machu Picchu is an extraordinary,unique, beautiful, and incredible scenic historical site. It's really as beautiful as pictures online. However, the city in Cusco is extremely overcooked. There are 6 thousand people visiting Machu Pichu daily. Whatsoever time you booked for your visit, you have to wait for your bus and stand a line with other other x hundreds of people. I had a ticket for 7 a.m., and i stood in line since 6:20 am. and arrived to Machu Pichu at 7:45 a.m., which is normal. I've hiked all circuits, including inka bridge and the highest mountain, Waynapicchu, which have 1600 stairs. That was really hard but worth it. You have to buy tickets ahead in advance online. The bus tickets each way cost $24 return per day. If you want to avoid that and reach the gate of Machu Pichu, you can walk to the hills 2+ hours, which i don't recommend. It's better to do down the hills on footpath from Machu Pichu. The cafeterias were so expensive at gates , small bottles of water, $2.15 and in Cusco city 2 litres cost $1.61 😃 it's definitely worth to see it once in life here in Peru. I am highly recommending it. There is so many insects and little mosquitos at Machu Picchu, the best is wearing trousers and still use repelent which you can buy absolutely anywhere in the city for 8 sols, the most powerful 30% is the small bottle,photo included here. Everywhere in the city is available to buy a passport stamp for 5 sol. It's not recommended to do that as some counties count this as illegal or failure of passport. I've travelled 99 countries worldwide, and nobody ever discovered some stamps like that as other countries have no clue, and with too many stamps in your passport, nobody will even notice. It's at your own risk.

text_clean: Machu Picchu ⛰ La forma más barata de visitar Machu Picchu es teniendo tiempo: ⛰ De Cusco tomar un transfer hasta Ollantaytambo temprano en la mañana y el trayecto dura 1 hora con 30 minutos. La agencia te recoge en el hospedaje. ⛰ Luego otro viaje en tren local y como es tan solicitado por los propios Peruanos, toca hacer la reserva con tiempo para poder encontrar cupos; la prioridad es para los locales. ⛰ Para subir a las ruinas, el paquete incluye la entrada pero si llegas por ti mismo; existen dos formas: la paga y la gratis. Puedes usar el bus que vale para extranjeros 12 dólares por trayecto. Aceptan efectivo y tarjeta. El viaje tarda 25 minutos. ⛰ La forma gratis es subir toda la montaña en un sendero señalizado y la subida dura casi tres horas. La bajada una hora y media. ⛰ Regresar a la hora pactada con la agencia para volver a Ollantaytambo y después a Cusco. ⛰ Para este plan las agencias cobran 200 soles e incluye: - Transporte ida y vuelta de Cusco a Ollantaytambo. - Transporte ida y vuelta en tren local. - Bus subida y bajada hasta ruinas de Machu Picchu. - Ingreso a Machu Picchu. - Guía profesional. ⛰ Toca contratar con cuidado porque a veces las agencias estafan dejando a la gente de pie en el tren durante el viaje. ⛰ Es poco común encontrar este tipo de paquetes porque les conviene vender el plan turístico que sale más del doble de costoso. ⛰ También puedes hacer el trekking por la hidroeléctrica y quedándote a dormir en el pueblo; es otro tipo de plan también económico. ⛰ Datos por si vas por tu cuenta en un sólo día: * Transfer ida y vuelta a Ollantaytambo: 25 ☀️ * Tren turístico hasta Aguascalientes: Desde 50 dólares el trayecto. * Bus ida y vuelta hasta ruinas: 12 dólares/trayecto. * Entrada a ruinas: 64 ☀️ para la comunidad andina. * Guía (opcional): 20 ☀️ si el grupo es de +de 8 personas. * Almuerzo en el pueblo: desde 25 ☀️.

La bajada una hora y media. ⛰ Regresar a la hora pactada con la agencia para volver a Ollantaytambo y después a Cusco. ⛰ Para este plan las agencias cobran 200 soles e incluye: - Transporte ida y vuelta de Cusco a Ollantaytambo. - Transporte ida y vuelta en tren local. - Bus subida y bajada hasta ruinas de Machu Picchu. - Ingreso a Machu Picchu. - Guía profesional. ⛰ Toca contratar con cuidado porque a veces las agencias estafan dejando a la gente de pie en el tren durante el viaje. ⛰ Es poco común encontrar este tipo de paquetes porque les conviene vender el plan turístico que sale más del doble de costoso. ⛰ También puedes hacer el trekking por la hidroeléctrica y quedándote a dormir en el pueblo; es otro tipo de plan también económico. ⛰ Datos por si vas por tu cuenta en un sólo día: * Transfer ida y vuelta a Ollantaytambo: 25 ☀️ * Tren turístico hasta Aguascalientes: Desde 50 dólares el trayecto. * Bus ida y vuelta hasta ruinas: 12 dólares/trayecto. * Entrada a ruinas: 64 ☀️ para la comunidad andina. * Guía (opcional): 20 ☀️ si el grupo es de +de 8 personas. * Almuerzo en el pueblo: desde 25 ☀️.
label: neutro

text_clean: The site itself is exactly what you see in the photos. The views around are nice. It's very crowded though, and buying tickets is a bureaucratic nightmare: You have to queue for a number that allows you to buy a ticket at a certain time. Then have to come back at that time, listen for your number (which is called out by some guy in Spanish to a crowd of hundreds), then queue again. Then you have to pick one of 6 routes and 3 additions, most of which will no longer be available in the upcoming days, depending on your luck. The routes that are available usually don't allow entrance to the actual site since those are booked way in advance via a terrible online system. If you want to go at the start of the year, you cannot book tickets in advance, because you can only by tickets for the current year. Also, this online system is down or unresponsive for most of January. Even though we booked through an agency 6 months in advance, they were not able to secure our tickets so we had to queue. In the end, our group were not able to actually visit the site, only take a photo from top.... There are so many nice things to see in Peru and South America... Including much larger and more impressive Inca settlements, which are much cheaper or even free to visit... It's really a shame the Peruvian makes it so difficult to visit this place. Unless you really really want to visit Machu Picchu, I would recommend spending your time elsewhere.

text_clean: Machu Picchu: A Must-See Wonder, with a Few Tips Machu Picchu truly lives up to the hype – it's a place of breathtaking beauty and incredible history. Hiring a guide at the bus station was the best decision we made. Our guide was not only incredibly knowledgeable, weaving tales of the Incas into the very stones, but she was also wonderful with our children and a whiz at getting those perfect family photos. A word of advice for those not accustomed to high altitudes: spend a day or two acclimating in Cusco beforehand. The city offers a wealth of activities and attractions, so you won't be bored. Trust me, it makes a huge difference at Machu Picchu. Speaking of getting to Machu Picchu, the bus situation from Aguas Calientes can be a bit chaotic. Signage isn't great, and boarding can feel rushed. We traveled with Inca Rail, and while the train itself was comfortable, the waiting area was overcrowded and a bit messy. Overall: Machu Picchu is an unforgettable experience. Just be prepared for the altitude and a bit of a scramble getting there. Manage expectations: The bus situation and train station can be less than ideal. Make sure tu buy your ticket in advance.

text_clean: Espectacular. Me esperaba un lugar más donde ver ruinas en mal estado de conservación, y debo admitir que me sorprendió muy gratamente. Es muy recomendable ir acompañado de un guía que te va explicando la historia del lugar y te hace la ruta más interesante. Aconsejo dar un paseo hasta la puerta del sol para tener una vista espectacular del recinto. La subida a WaynaPicchu tiene mucho desnivel pero en ningún momento tuvo la sensación de peligro durante su ascenso. Debes ir con cuidado y mirando bien donde pisas. A tener en cuenta: los domingos los peruanos tienen la entrada gratuita por lo que es probable que encontréis más gente durante vuestra visita. El uso de los baños tiene un coste de 2 soles, y sólo se encuentran en la puerta del recinto antes de entrar. También hay una tienda de regalos y pueden ponerte un sello en tu pasaporte a modo de souvenir si así lo deseas. Los autobuses tardan unos 25 minutos en subir y 20 en bajar y salen cada vez que se completan y tiene un coste de 24USD por trayecto (en diciembre de 2018). Casi imprescindible llevar protección solar para evitar quemaduras durante la visita.

text_clean: Machu Picchu - 10 things that may help to make your trip more pleasurable updated: 25 September 2022 1. Book entry tickets well in advance - there is a daily quota of 2,000 people only 2. Decide on pace of travel - day trip from Cusco is doable but it’s still at least a 12-hour ordeal door to door. To have a less stressful visit if time allows, stay in fun Aguas Calientes to chill for an evening or 2 before embarking on your choice of transportation is a pretty good idea. 3. Decide on how you want to get there - a). Hiking all the way (28 miles for 4d3n) b). Train all the way (Inkarail / Perurail for 1.5 hrs) c). Train and Hike combo (train then hike the last 8 miles) d). Bus 4. Decide on when you want to get there - a). For Sunrise, be prepared to stay a night in Machu Picchu (national park opens at 6am) b). For Sunset, time yourself to find a nice seat facing west near the sundial. Remember you have 3 hours inside the park and you will need to get yourself out after sunset. 5. Local tour guide is essential - the last chance to find them if you do not already have one will be in the bus queue from Machu Picchu train station or outside the national park. Apart from facts and myths you can read up prior to the visit, the guide will be able to point out interesting facts such as: best spots to take photographs / the stones that look like a llama. 6. Separate bookings / time restricted visits - a). Visit to the sundial is included in the tickets for national park entry but it’s only opened from 7-10am daily b). Wayna Picchu (Young Mountain) , Hucchu Picchu (Baby Mountain) & Gran Caverna: this requires booking separately. There are 2 groups of 200 pax daily capacity. Round trip time takes around 2 hrs / 45 min / 4 hrs respectively. 7. Mosquito repellent: this is absolutely essential if you do not want to remember the breath taking view of Machu Picchu by the itchy mozzie scars! These bugs are tiny black things, and they draw blood in nano seconds! Army grade DEET is recommended especially if you are a sunrise / sunset romantic, or if you intend to do any form of hiking. The bites may only appear up to 36 hours later! 8. Climate check: a). check whether but be prepared to encounter all 4 seasons on your visit b). check political actions - as protests can shut down all roads and transportations! 9. Be prepared to queue: no pain no gain! 10. Altitude: if you are like me whose normal inhabitant is at sea level, climatise yourself gently. Cusco is at 3400m and although Michu Picchu is at a lower altitude of 2400m, strolling can still be taxing at times! Hope you have a wonderful trip like I did!

text_clean: Don’t bother. The site is closed and you can’t even walk along the base of the wall. You pretty much can get to a fence near where the entrance would have been. It sounds like it will be literally years at best before the site reopens. None of the guides or tour operators or people running the site want to admit that their ‘premiere site’ for northern Peru is closed to tourism for possibly a decade. I would vaguely guess they will need to build a roof over the entire structure at some point to keep stormwater from destabilizing the wall any further. Unless the want to tear sections of the wall down, put a drainage system behind the wall, and rebuild it with a modern support / drainage system behind the walls. Anyways this section of the country is fantastic, and you should totally visit it. But don’t pretend you are going to be able to walk around the top of Kuelap anytime this decade.

text_clean: Machu Picchu, término quechua cuyo significado es Montaña vieja. Hoy es universalmente conocida, tanto por sus imponentes restos arqueológicos, como por su incomparable ubicación, al borde de un abismo en cuyo fondo corren las caudalosas aguas del Río Urubamba. Los boletos tienen precios diferenciados, para peruanos, países miembros de la comunidad andina, y otros extranjeros. Pueden llegar caminando desde Machupichu pueblo (creo que dura una hora el camino), o en bus que cobra $12 dólares por trayecto me parece demasiado caro, pero es eso o caminar, los boletos los compran cerca de la estación de tren. Hay 4 circuitos para escoger, nosotros escogimos el circuito 1 y vimos gran parte de la ciudad. No es obligatorio ir con guía. Nosotros no íbamos a contratar guía, pero fue necesario porque fui con un pie fracturado y la guía fue de gran ayuda para decirnos por donde ir, nos explicó todo y nos hizo buenas fotos. Fue una excelente decisión haber contratado a Graciela (está en las fotos, se las recomiendo totalmente). Nos hizo un tour privado por $50 dólares. Hay algunas zonas que se pueden visitar antes de las 10am (reposo del sol) y otras que se pueden visitar después de las 10am. No se puede comer dentro del recinto. Tampoco se puede usar paraguas, lleven su chubasquero porque posiblemente lo necesiten. Afuera hay un restaurante, no se que vendan ni los precios porque no lo visitamos. En el recinto se respira una paz inexplicable, disfruten su estadía.

text_clean: Huaca Pucllana came recommended from a tour guide. He said the restaurant next to it was amazing. He’s 2 for 2!! So Huaca Pucllana - you get queued up in a group, but the people never stop coming so you don’t wait long. They do English groups and Spanish groups. The guides are great , funny knowledgeable , and good at herding cats. If you’re reading this before you go / bring stuff to protect yourself from the sun. A hat, sunscreen, etc and bring water. There no shade and it’s hot. The day was cool in Lima but kinda hot here despite being right in town. It’s also dusty so don’t wear your finest. Ok, with that out of the way… awesome site and incredible history - you can walk through a pre Incan pyramid in the middle of the city !!! It’s all made from adobe - Sun dried clay - so it’s dusty. They do a great job keeping it interesting and as you explore you learn so much and see new things. I Wondered if it was going to be repetitive- but it was great. You also get a great view of the city - I didn’t think it was that high up but it totally is. Worth it for the views alone.

text_clean: First advise: arrive the day before you want to visit, stay in the small village, get up really early and take the first bus to this site to avoid the large crowds. I was there early and could enjoy the remains of what once must have been a beautiful well organised Inca citadel. It lies on a ridge 2430m above sealevel. As the Inca's did not keep written language records the purpose of the site has long been unknown but is now believed to have been the estate of Inca emperor Pachacuti It must have been a tremendous engineering and construction task to build this and a challenge to provide food and fresh water to it's inhabitants And it reminds us that no civilisation is forever.

text_clean: Machu Picchu obra maestra de la arquitectura y la ingeniería de los incas. Declarado como santuario histórico de Perú en 1981, forma parte de la Unesco y es una de las 7 maravillas del mundo declarado en el año 2007. Es un lugar súper hermoso lleno de mucha historia y cultura inca. Fue usado como palacio y santuario del inca Pachacutec. En él se encuentran el templo al sol, a la luna, y ﻿el templo de las 3 ventanas donde rendían culto a sus dioses. Los incas que vivían acá estuvieron dedicados a la agricultura, sembraban en los andenes o terrazas que acá se encuentran. Adicional a ello era un puesto de vigilancia o control del imperio inca con el antisuyo.. Era un punto central y estratégico ya que en el se conectan los mas de 7 caminos del inca, por donde se comunicaban con otros pueblos.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Anímate a visitar las enigmáticas Líneas de Nasca Las Líneas de Nasca son unos curiosos geoglifos (figuras construidas en cerros usando piedras). Se encuentran localizadas en las Pampas de Jumana, en el desierto de Nasca, entre esta ciudad y Palpa en Perú. Algunas de las líneas poseen un ancho de entre los 40 y los 210 centímetros y se extienden por 520 km², y otras incluso se prolongan hasta un área de 800 km². El principal atractivo son las Líneas de Nasca, las cuales se pueden ver desde una avioneta o subiendo a las colinas cercanas, dado que estas se han mantenido en buen estado debido al buen clima que tiene la ciudad. Asimismo, puedes visitar museos y los alrededores del lugar. El costo aproximado del pasaje en avioneta bordea los USD 100 dólares por persona, a lo que se le suma unos 30 soles por impuestos aeroportuarios. Conviene hacer el recorrido aéreo durante las primeras horas de la mañana, ya que es cuando suele estar más despejado y hay menos viento, lo que favorecerá tu visibilidad y te permitirá tomar mejores fotos. En promedio, el recorrido dura unos 30 minutos, y en el trayecto podrás observar 12 figuras, entre ellas el mono, la araña, el pelícano, entre otros. No te preocupes mucho por el clima, no por nada Nazca es conocida como «la ciudad del eterno verano». El clima es soleado y seco durante todo el año, con una temperatura promedio de 23°C aunque puede llegar hasta los 30°C entre los meses de enero y marzo. Si vas a sobrevolar las Líneas de Nazca es importante que lleves una casaca cortavientos, pues puede haber vientos huracanados en el desierto. Recuerda que el vuelo dura unos 30 minutos aproximadamente (lleva pastillas para el mareo, por si acaso). Al ser un clima soleado y cálido, no olvides tu bloqueador, lentes de sol y otros objetos “veraniegos”. Si eres de los que temen las turbulencias, te recomendamos volar las Líneas de Nazca por la mañana, y así evitar los fuertes vientos de la tarde. Que te diviertas..!!! Si te gusto la información dale "Like"

text_clean: My Peru experience was amazing and undertaking the Inca Trail to Machu Picchu one of the most wonderful experiences of my life. Camping and waking up to such beautiful mountain views in the Andes, will stay with me forever. It's a tough walk but one I'd recommend to anyone wanting to see and feel some of our beautiful planet. To finally arrive at the Sun Gate and see Machu Picchu for the first time, at dawn, just, wow. The amusing part is then walking around this breathtaking place, after 5 days of basically living rough, filthy and covered in bug bites amongst all the clean tourists who have got the train up, makes you feel quite self conscious lol!! So I'd say walking the Inca Trail is the ultimate way to experience this, if you can. If not, a day trip via train is certainly to be recommended just to get to see Machu Picchu itself.

text_clean: Machu Picchu es maravilloso, llegué con mucha Niebla a las 6:30 am . La ciudad se veía muy mística, después de subir Wayna Picchu la ciudadela estaba despejada, se apreciaba todo. No hay mejor sensación que caminar por los restos arqueológicos incas y aprender cómo ellos vivían. Es increíble cómo hacían encajar las rocas a la perfección en el estilo inca imperial. Puedes visitar el templo del Sol, el templo al cóndor, la caseta del guardian, entre otros. Definitivamente no dan ganas de irse. Lo único malo es que el baño está fuera del parque, y si has comprado la entrada a Machu Picchu (sin Wayna) puedes salir solo una vez y no te dejan volver a entrar. Además te cobran por usar el baño, lo que encuentro un robo considerando que la entrada es bastante cara para un extranjero y ni siquiera incluye un guía.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: n un desierto de Nazca, se encuentran estos enigmáticos dibujos y líneas que los arqueólogos las denominan Geogliflos y que hasta el día de hoy no encuentran una explicación del como y el porque fueron construidos, solo como un detalle no son visibles desde el nivel del suelo, fueron hechos para ser vistos desde lo alto y las evidencias de las culturas de esa época, evidenciaban de que hubieran necesitado tecnologías superiores que no les correspondería ni a la época ni lugar. Según información de nuestro guía, las líneas rectas son tan perfectas que su desviación geométrica medida, no llegan al 2 por 1000 (2 metros por cada kilómetro) La Líneas de Nazca más representativas son los figuras de animales, entre ellos: colibríes, cóndores, garza, la grulla, el pelícano, gaviota, mono, araña, caracol, perro con patas y cola larga, figura antropomorfa, dos llamas, etc. Reptiles como el lagarto, cortado por la carretera Panamericana Sur, la iguana, lagartija, y una serpiente. Además de Líneas rectas a manera de pistas de aterrizaje, mezclado con espirales. Su perfecto estado de conservación se debe María Reiche Neumann, alemana que dedico su vida a limpiar y literalmente barrer las líneas para evitar su destrucción y es gracias a ella que la Unesco inscribió a “ Las líneas y Geogliflos de Nazca y de Pampas de Jumana” como Patrimonio de la Humanidad.

text_clean: Una maravilla del mundo Realmente fantástico Machu Picchu es muy hermoso Se llega desde aguas calientes con un bus Los guías son amables y te brindan toda la información necesaria para un excelente recorrido, pero a estar muy atentos para aprender de toda su historia El recorrido es seguro pero en lluvias llevar buenos zapatos porque te puedes resbalar Respecto a las fotos lo puedes hacer en diferentes partes del recorrido, todo es hermoso Recorrido desde Cuzco por tu propia cuenta: primero de Cusco tomas un carro hasta Ollantaytambo, cobran aproximadamente 10 soles y se llega en dos horas, después haces cola aproximadamente tres horas para subir al tren, si no alcanzas por uedes esperar al sgte tren o ir a la sgte estación con un auto que cobra 10 soles, en el tren ya pagas tú pasaje que es 13 soles, es el tren municipal por lo tanto puedes ir de pie, pero existe la posiblidad de tomar el tren turístico que cuesta 70 dólares; el tren te lleva hasta aguas calientes, cuando llegas puedes ir a comprar tu boleto de entrada a Machu Picchu al ministerio de cultura, atienden hasta las 10 pm, hay diferentes tarifas, adulto 67, escolar y universitario a 35 soles; y el horario depende de uno y de la disponibilidad. Después compras tu boleto para el bus, caminas un poco y está la boletería. Todo es con un día de anticipación. Después de comprar tus boletos, al día sgte abordas el bus hacia el santuario, llegas en aproximadamente 30 min, de ahí en adelante sería recomendable contratar un guía para aprender y conocer de la cultura inca. El recorrido dura dos horas Machu Picchu es ceja de selva por lo tanto el clima es cálido Llevar agua para el recorrido e ir muy ligero y con lo necesario como un sombrero, casaca impermeable o un plástico para la lluvia.

text_clean: Climbing Machu Picchu Mountain on My 49th Birthday Decided to take on the Machu Picchu peak hike to mark my 49th birthday—honestly, I thought I’d only go halfway since I wasn’t fully prepared and didn’t carry enough water. But once we started the climb, especially up those endless stone stairs, things got real. It was tough, and more than once I felt like giving up. You also have to reach certain control points at scheduled times, or you risk being turned back—so there’s a bit of pressure to keep moving. Thankfully, the weather was in our favor. Even with just a little water, I managed to push through. There are roughly 1,600 uneven stone steps. The first half doesn’t offer many views and feels like a slog. But once you reach the steeper parts, you’re rewarded with stunning vistas of ancient Inca villages and lush valleys. And when you finally reach the summit—wow. A full 360° panoramic view, clear skies, and the raw beauty of the Andes makes every aching muscle worth it. My advice? Go slow. Breathe. Take your time. The last 400 steps will test you—they’re steep, and the altitude hits hard. But don’t give up. Just keep moving at your pace. The top is unforgettable Carry enough water n energy bars to celebrate at the top. Going up it took 2 hrs coming down and is also not easy took toll on knees 1 hrs total round trip 3 hrs

text_clean: El Circuito Mágico del Agua en Lima me dejó asombrado. Es una idea diferente de un parque en comparación con otros lugares que había visitado antes. He visto espacios con fuentes en el pasado, pero nunca había experimentado un lugar tan impresionante y extenso centrado completamente en el agua. Este lugar es simplemente genial y versátil. Es perfecto para llevar a los niños, visitar con amigos, tu pareja o la familia. Además, es un paraíso para los amantes de la fotografía, ya que todas las fuentes tienen formas y tamaños diferentes, y el entorno ofrece muchas oportunidades para tomar fotos espectaculares. El Circuito Mágico del Agua no solo ofrece fuentes, sino también áreas para sentarse y relajarse, leer un libro o simplemente disfrutar del entorno tranquilo. Además, hay juegos para niños, lo que lo convierte en un lugar divertido y educativo para ellos. Aunque no encontré opciones de bebidas calientes, no te preocupes, ya que encontrarás carros de comida y otras opciones de alimentos. La entrada al parque es bastante asequible, solo cuatro soles, y los servicios, como los baños, son igualmente económicos. Sin embargo, te recomendaría llevar algo para abrigarte, ya que el clima en Lima puede ser agradable hasta cierta hora, pero en la noche tiende a enfriarse bastante. Esto es especialmente importante si planeas quedarte para el espectáculo de luces, que comienza a las 7:15 p. m. El espectáculo, aunque corto (quizás no más de 10 minutos), realmente vale la pena. En resumen, el Circuito Mágico del Agua es un lugar super recomendado, especialmente para visitar en familia, y estoy seguro de que tanto los niños como los adultos disfrutarán de eventos como el impresionante espectáculo de luces que ofrece este lugar único.

text_clean: MARAVILLOSO, lugar para disfrutar en familia, amigos. Las playas son maravillosas, puedes nadar y disfrutar de la arena fina! Excepto playa roja que no esta permitido ingresar, solo puedes mirar y siempre con cuidado ya que las olas en cualquier momento rompen con fuerza y hace unos meses fui testigo de un accidente ocurrido ahí. No esta permitido llevar comidas, ni botellas no reutilizables, cañitas, para evitar la contaminación, pueden llevar un poco de fruta pero no boten la basura al mar y tampoco dejen sus bolsas en la area, al salir pueden regresar con sus bolsas de basura para echarlas en el bote de basura que cuentan en la parte del ingreso.

text_clean: Lugar increíble, caminata nivel 2. Debes hidratarte pero si te ven agua te la quitan, llévala en tu mochila. Por ningún motivo dejes basura, restos de alimentos ni te lleves piedras. No cortes las plantas y no toques o pintes en ningún lugar. Hay un par de puntos cerrados por visitantes irresponsables. Intenta tomar fotos de manera rápida ya que hay muchísima gente esperando y si te tardas mucho no das oportunidad a todos. También corres el riesgo de que algún turista se meta entre tú y tu cámara SIEMPRE. Formate con tiempo para tomar el bus que te sube ya que tú boleto pasa 2 filtros y si no, corres el riesgo de perder tu lugar. Ve al baño antes de entrar al recorrido ya que dentro no hay baños hasta volver a salir, cobran 2 soles a esta fecha y lleva monedas de 1 sol para pagar y para las máquinas dispensadoras de agua, a veces no tienen cambio. IMPORTANTE tomar una pastilla si tienes vértigo ya que son 20 mint de curvas extremas y rápidas, lo agradecerás al llegar. Hay 3 circuitos, el más recomendable es el 2. Disfrútalo mucho 😊❣️

text_clean: Plaza Mayor de Lima , realmente hermosa. Bien cuidada y con grandes diseños. Actualmente se encuentra bastante custodiada para evitar marchas o algún tema de inseguridad (se han desplazado bastante personal de seguridad y policías para mantener el orden). Aunque son amables y permiten que el público ingrese para pasear y tomarse fotos. Fui un día domingo y me sorprendió cuan ordenado estaba todo. Muy tranquilo. Tambien hacen paseos en caballo con carruaje, es muy elegante y bonito aunque hay muchas críticas por el maltrato animal evidenciado en redes sociales, en esta ocasión pude visualizar tranquilidad en los animalitos y sobretodo su espacio para que tomen agua, el costo del paseo es de 7 soles por persona. Igual, antes de contratar el servicio verifiquen que sean buenos y respetuosos con los caballitos, hay que consumir lo bueno, sin dañar a terceros.
